hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Three failed lottery attempts with no bridge means forced departure after OPT exhaustion; the cap-exempt employer bridge option exists but is rarely surfaced by HR unless the employee asks directly
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Any F-1 OPT holder whose employer does not proactively discuss contingency planning

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Using a university-affiliated entity as the cap-exempt sponsor
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Not all university-affiliated entities qualify — for-profit spinouts and hospitals that primarily deliver care may fail the 'primary purpose' test, even when they appear institutionally connected to a university
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Candidates who assume that any connection to a university confers cap-exempt status, leading to wasted time and legal fees pursuing an ineligible path

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Treating immigration status as a personal achievement or merit outcome
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: When the lottery fails, it registers as a personal failure rather than a statistical event — generating shame and depression disproportionate to what actually happened
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: F-1 students who have succeeded through meritocratic systems and interpret the random lottery as a judgment on their worth

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Obtaining US green card and later living or working abroad
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The US taxes worldwide income for green card holders regardless of where they live. This creates significant long-term tax liability for those who want to return home or work internationally after getting GC.
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: GC holders who plan to eventually return to home country or take international assignments

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Stopping work when the EAD card has not arrived by the expiry date
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: If a timely-filed receipt notice exists, stopping work is unnecessary and creates an unexplained employment gap — but most employees and employers do not know this protection exists
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Any STEM OPT applicant whose employer HR is unfamiliar with timely-filed protections and demands the employee stop working

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Relying solely on PGWP as the immigration pathway
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: PGWP policy instability (multiple major adjustments in 2024–2025) means the pathway can change after enrollment, leaving students with fewer options than expected
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: International students who chose their program primarily for PGWP eligibility without building parallel immigration buffers (e.g., Canadian work experience, PNP pathways)

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Waiting until the final year of STEM OPT to raise H-1B sponsorship with employer
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Internal employer approval processes can take months; by the time approval is granted, the H-1B registration window may have already passed
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: STEM OPT holders at large corporations or companies with bureaucratic HR and legal processes
hidden_tradeoffs._source: zhihu_F_1留学生从OPT到H_1B_中间有哪些需要注意的关键节点.txt

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Waiting through all three STEM OPT lottery attempts before exploring alternatives
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: If all three fail, the person exits OPT status with little or no preparation time for Canada PR, O-1 accumulation, or NIW filing — compressing a multi-year process into a crisis window
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Anyone who treats Plan B as a fallback rather than a parallel track
hidden_tradeoffs._source: zhihu_H_1B三次抽签都没中的话_留在美国还有哪些合法途径.txt

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Relying solely on a high English IELTS score and ignoring French
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Missing a ~50-point gain from French CLB 7 that requires only B1-level fluency — a level far below professional French proficiency — at relatively low cost and time investment
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Candidates whose CRS is 10–50 points below recent cutoffs who assume French is out of reach without near-native fluency
hidden_tradeoffs._source: zhihu_加拿大Express_Entry的CRS分数怎么计算_有哪些可以主动提升的因素.txt

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: 依赖联邦Express Entry综合类抽签，不考虑省提名
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: CRS分数处于490-530门槛以下的申请人可能在PGWP有效期内长期等待而无法获得ITA，错过最佳申请窗口
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: CRS分数在450-490区间、没有法语或Job Offer加分杠杆的理工科硕士毕业生
hidden_tradeoffs._source: zhihu_在加拿大读完研究生_毕业后移民有多现实_从PGWP到PR需要几年.txt

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Assuming US citizenship unlocks fast parental reunion
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: US citizenship does remove the numeric cap for parent sponsorship (IR-5, ~1 year processing), but getting to citizenship typically requires green card first — the bottleneck is the green card wait, not the citizenship itself
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Anyone planning around the 'eventually I'll be a citizen and sponsor my parents' assumption without accounting for the intervening decade
hidden_tradeoffs._source: zhihu_在美国还是加拿大工作_对国内父母将来的团聚有多大影响.txt

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Choosing Canada for PR certainty
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: PGWP holders face a widespread employer misconception that 'work permit = requires sponsorship' — many companies self-screen out PGWP candidates without ever asking, meaning job search is harder than the legal status would suggest
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Recent graduates in Canada on PGWP who assume their open work authorization removes them from visa-related hiring friction
hidden_tradeoffs._source: zhihu_美国H_1B和加拿大PR_对于中国背景的留学生_哪条路更现实.txt

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Switching employers during green card processing
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: May require restarting the PERM labor certification process, effectively losing years of queue position for parts of the green card process (though I-140 priority date may be preserved if already approved)
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Anyone in EB-2 or EB-3 employer-sponsored green card process who receives a better job offer mid-process
hidden_tradeoffs._source: zhihu_美国绿卡排期对印度_中国大陆出生的申请人有多严重.txt

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Getting Canadian PR and then working in the US
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Canadian PR requires maintaining residency (730 days in 5 years); working in the US long-term can put PR status at risk — this was raised as a question with no clear resolution in the thread
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: People who hope to maintain Canadian PR as a backup while maximizing US career earnings
hidden_tradeoffs._source: canada_加拿大vs美国移民路线的真实对比_从两边都走过的角度.txt

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Not explaining PGWP clearly upfront in application materials
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: HR at smaller companies may screen out candidates pre-interview on the assumption that any work permit requires employer-sponsored LMIA or similar burden, even when PGWP requires nothing from the employer
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: International graduates applying to companies with limited immigration HR knowledge, particularly outside of major tech firms
hidden_tradeoffs._source: canada_在加拿大找第一份工作的经验_PGWP身份怎么向雇主解释.txt

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Taking a cap-exempt research university role at lower salary
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Salary drop of ~30% is real, but the tradeoff includes no lottery risk, faster green card timeline, and lower psychological burden — total career value may be higher depending on the individual's risk pricing
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: High earners at large tech companies who anchor their decision purely on current compensation without modeling the value of visa certainty

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Avoiding research into Plan B options to avoid feeling like a failure
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: When the lottery fails, the person is forced to make high-stakes decisions (Canada immigration application, job search, relocation) under time pressure and emotional distress, with no prior research — worse outcomes than if they had prepared calmly
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Anyone whose identity is heavily invested in the US path; particularly common among those who framed the US as the primary goal from the start of their academic journey
hidden_tradeoffs._source: immigration_关于H_1B抽签后身份焦虑_说几句心里话.txt

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Exceeding the 90-day unemployment limit without knowing it
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The violation can surface during H-1B RFE adjudication, long after OPT has ended, creating retroactive immigration consequences
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Anyone who had a prolonged job search gap on OPT and later applies for H-1B status
hidden_tradeoffs._source: us_visa_OPT期间换工作完整攻略_我踩过的所有坑.txt

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Waiting for federal all-program draws instead of pursuing PNP
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Missing lower-cutoff provincial pathways that require provincial ties or job offers but are realistically accessible
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Applicants from high-competition countries who are close to but not at federal cutoff and are passively waiting

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Prioritizing immigration certainty over maximum salary
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: A 25-35% salary reduction compared to Bay Area is a concrete, compounding financial cost over a career — the psychological relief of immigration certainty is real but the dollar gap is also real and not recoverable through immigration status alone
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: High earners in senior tech roles or those with significant student debt who need maximum income in early career years

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Mentioning future sponsorship needs (or implying them) in cover letters
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Triggers employer rejection even when no sponsorship is actually needed — PGWP holders are treated as sponsorship burdens based on wording alone
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: PGWP holders who are not yet thinking about PR pathway and use imprecise language about their status

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Over-explaining visa complexity unprompted during early interview stages
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Recruiter becomes risk-averse or confused before evaluating the candidate's technical merit, effectively pre-screening out a qualified candidate on administrative grounds
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Anxious candidates who front-load visa disclosure as a defense mechanism against rejection

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Assuming university international office guidance is complete and accurate on OPT rules
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Multiple commenters (including a 156-upvote comment) report that international offices glossed over the 90-day unemployment rule entirely, leaving students unaware of a critical compliance threshold
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who rely solely on their DSO or international student office without independently verifying OPT employment rules

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Assuming cap-gap protection is automatic after H-1B lottery selection
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Students whose OPT EAD expires before the petition filing date have no cap-gap bridge and may fall out of status without realizing it
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: F-1 students with short OPT windows (e.g., late graduation dates, limited OPT start flexibility) who enter the H-1B lottery without checking EAD expiry alignment

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Filing under a generic SOC code for convenience
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Generic codes like 'computer programmer' attract higher USCIS scrutiny and RFE rates for roles that are actually specialty occupations under more specific codes
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Data scientists, ML engineers, and other emerging tech roles that straddle multiple SOC categories

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Choosing a 1-year Canadian graduate diploma to save time or money
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: A 1-year program yields only a 1-year PGWP — leaving very little runway to accumulate Canadian work experience, meet Express Entry CRS thresholds, and apply for PR before status expires
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who prioritize program cost or speed over immigration strategy

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Waiting until well after graduation to apply for PGWP
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: A subsequent policy announcement can change eligibility or duration rules before the application is submitted, even if the student was eligible at graduation
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who graduate but delay application due to job searching, travel, or uncertainty about their eligibility

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Using a generic template for the I-983 Training Plan
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: USCIS may issue a Notice of Intent to Deny (NOID), requiring a burdensome response and potentially jeopardizing STEM OPT approval
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who underestimate the I-983 as a formality and rely on shared or downloaded templates
hidden_tradeoffs._source: zhihu_F_1留学生从OPT到H_1B_中间有哪些需要注意的关键节点.txt

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Pursuing O-1 or EB-1A as a lottery alternative
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: These paths require years of documented achievement that cannot be manufactured on short notice; candidates who begin building this profile late in their OPT window are unlikely to qualify in time
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Candidates with strong but undocumented profiles who did not strategically accumulate evidence (publications, awards, peer review) early in their careers
hidden_tradeoffs._source: zhihu_H_1B三次抽签都没中的话_留在美国还有哪些合法途径.txt

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Focusing exclusively on federal Express Entry when CRS is below 450
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Prolonged profile sitting without draws while PNP pathways that could yield +600 points go uninvestigated
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Candidates with lower CRS scores who are unaware that PNP is an active parallel strategy rather than a fallback
hidden_tradeoffs._source: zhihu_加拿大Express_Entry的CRS分数怎么计算_有哪些可以主动提升的因素.txt

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Choosing Canada for parental reunion via PR sponsorship lottery
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The annual PR parent sponsorship lottery has only ~20–30% selection rate; relying on it as the primary reunion strategy is unreliable; Super Visa is more dependable for actual co-residence
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Canadian PRs or citizens who assume parent PR sponsorship is a straightforward, accessible process
hidden_tradeoffs._source: zhihu_在美国还是加拿大工作_对国内父母将来的团聚有多大影响.txt

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Comparing US vs. Canada on raw salary alone
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Family-stage economics — childcare costs, parental leave, child benefits — can significantly close or reverse the income gap, but this only becomes visible when family formation actually occurs
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Single people in their mid-20s making the comparison without modeling a 5–10 year life horizon that includes family costs
hidden_tradeoffs._source: zhihu_美国H_1B和加拿大PR_对于中国背景的留学生_哪条路更现实.txt

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Choosing Canada PR over continued US H-1B
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Faster permanent status (14 months cited) but ~25% salary reduction; the psychological relief is real but the financial cost is concrete and long-term
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Mid-career professionals with families who prioritize stability over maximizing US tech compensation
hidden_tradeoffs._source: zhihu_美国绿卡排期对印度_中国大陆出生的申请人有多严重.txt

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Switching employers on H-1B
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The 60-day grace period for job changes creates severe negotiating disadvantage — you cannot easily walk away from a bad offer or take time to find the right role without risking status
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: H-1B workers in toxic work environments or those who want to be selective in job searching
hidden_tradeoffs._source: canada_加拿大vs美国移民路线的真实对比_从两边都走过的角度.txt

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Submitting I-983 after starting work at a new STEM OPT employer
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Work performed before I-983 is submitted and E-Verify enrollment is confirmed may be unauthorized, creating a status violation even if the employer is eventually compliant
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: STEM OPT holders whose new employers are cooperative but unaware of the pre-start-date requirement
hidden_tradeoffs._source: us_visa_OPT期间换工作完整攻略_我踩过的所有坑.txt

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Obtaining Canadian PR
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: A high-upvote comment raises the question of whether PR can be maintained while working in the US short-term — suggesting people discover after obtaining PR that travel and residency obligations may conflict with US career opportunities
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Anyone who obtains Canadian PR but then receives a strong US job offer or wants to spend time working in the US

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Staying on H-1B rather than pivoting to Canada after repeated lottery failures
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Each additional year on STEM OPT waiting for lottery consumes irreplaceable time; if the 3-year STEM OPT cap is reached without H-1B approval, the person must leave the US with no status buffer remaining
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: STEM OPT holders who keep reapplying to H-1B without a contingency plan

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Not paying for H-1B premium processing
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Standard processing leaves a prolonged uncertainty window during which the applicant cannot confirm their work authorization status for the upcoming fiscal year
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Applicants whose employers are unwilling to absorb the premium processing fee and who have no savings buffer for job disruption
hidden_tradeoffs._source: zhihu_F_1留学生从OPT到H_1B_中间有哪些需要注意的关键节点.txt

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Defaulting to EB-2 employer-sponsored path without evaluating EB-1A
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Missing a self-petition option that bypasses PERM and has a shorter backlog — EB-1A is widely unknown in the community despite being potentially accessible to many STEM researchers
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: China-born and India-born applicants with strong research or achievement records who were never told EB-1A existed as an option
hidden_tradeoffs._source: zhihu_美国绿卡排期对印度_中国大陆出生的申请人有多严重.txt

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Accepting a Canadian job for PGWP/PR purposes
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Canadian work experience is weighted more heavily than international experience in Express Entry CRS scoring, meaning years spent working in the US before moving to Canada may not transfer as efficiently as expected for PR points
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Professionals who worked in the US for years before pivoting to Canada and expect that experience to be fully credited

failure_patterns.pattern: Dropping below full-time enrollment without DSO authorization because all desired courses were full
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students and even academic advisors assume that course unavailability is a reasonable exception, but it is not listed among the four permitted RCL exceptions.
failure_patterns.consequence: The student falls out of F-1 status and must apply for reinstatement through USCIS, which is costly, time-consuming, and not guaranteed.
warning_signs: Student reports courses are full and asks if they can take fewer credits this semester
Academic advisor recommends reduced load without mentioning DSO consultation

failure_patterns.pattern: Submitting the PGWP application after the 180-day window has closed
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students conflate the 180-day clock starting from graduation ceremony or diploma receipt rather than the letter of completion; some wait for 'official' documents that are not required
failure_patterns.consequence: Permanent loss of PGWP eligibility for that program — no appeals or extensions; must leave Canada or apply for a different status
warning_signs: Student says 'I'm waiting for my diploma to arrive before applying'
Student graduated more than 4 months ago and has not yet applied
Student is unsure when their letter of completion was issued

failure_patterns.pattern: Counting co-op work terms as CEC-eligible experience
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students assume any authorized Canadian work experience counts; the distinction between full-time student status and post-graduation status is not intuitive
failure_patterns.consequence: Student believes they are CEC-eligible earlier than they are; submits profile prematurely or plans timeline incorrectly, potentially leading to application refusal
warning_signs: Student says 'I've been working in Canada for 2 years' but includes co-op terms in that count
Student's calculated eligibility date is before or immediately at graduation

failure_patterns.pattern: Counting co-op or self-employment hours toward CEC eligibility
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students confuse 'Canadian work experience' broadly with CEC-eligible experience specifically; co-op is real work done in Canada but is explicitly excluded by IRCC rules
failure_patterns.consequence: Filing a CEC application that fails the eligibility check, resulting in refusal, a gap in status if PGWP expires, and wasted application fees
warning_signs: Student says 'I've already worked in Canada during school so I qualify for CEC'
Student includes co-op hours in their 1,560-hour calculation
Student has not read the CEC-specific eligibility requirements beyond general 'Canadian work experience' language

failure_patterns.pattern: Verifying PGWP eligibility once at enrollment and never rechecking
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students treat eligibility as a static fact — they confirm it at application time and assume it remains true throughout their studies; they do not anticipate that IRCC can overhaul eligible fields lists mid-program
failure_patterns.consequence: Students in programs removed in the June 2025 overhaul discover ineligibility only when applying for PGWP after graduation, with no time to switch programs and no grandfathering protection if their study permit postdates November 2024
warning_signs: Student cites eligibility information from a blog post or institutional brochure published before June 2025
Student is enrolled in a transport, agriculture, or environmental studies program with a post-November 2024 study permit

failure_patterns.pattern: Enrolling in a diploma program without verifying PGWP field eligibility under the new (post-June 2025) rules
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students rely on peer advice, recruitment agents, or institution marketing that predates the June 2025 rule changes — and institutions may not proactively disclose eligibility risk
failure_patterns.consequence: No PGWP issued after graduation; student has no legal work authorization in Canada and must leave or find an alternative permit, effectively losing the investment in Canadian education as an immigration pathway
warning_signs: Choosing a program based on a friend's PGWP experience from before June 2025
Institution or agent says the program 'has always been PGWP eligible' without referencing the current IRCC list
Program is in transportation, certain agriculture fields, or traditional trades

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating Express Entry and PNP as sequential rather than parallel strategies
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students learn about Express Entry first (it's more widely discussed) and only investigate PNP after failing to receive an ITA — by which time PGWP validity may be critically short
failure_patterns.consequence: Lost 6–12 months of application time; may require bridge work permit or even departure from Canada while PR is processed
warning_signs: Student has been in the Express Entry pool 6+ months with no ITA and has not yet explored any PNP stream
Student's PGWP expires within 18 months and they have no active PNP application

failure_patterns.pattern: Dropping below full-time enrollment and assuming the DSO can fix it retroactively
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students conflate the DSO's administrative authority with legal authority — DSOs can update SEVIS records but cannot retroactively authorize an RCL that was never approved before the drop occurred
failure_patterns.consequence: Student is immediately out of status from the date of the unauthorized drop; must file USCIS reinstatement application; may be barred from re-entry if they travel internationally
warning_signs: Student contacts DSO after already submitting course withdrawal
Student asks 'can you backdate the approval?' or 'can we say I told you earlier?'
Student was unaware they needed DSO approval before dropping

failure_patterns.pattern: Starting work on the OPT approval notice before the physical EAD card arrives
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students conflate USCIS approval of the application with work authorization; the I-797C receipt or approval notice feels like permission because it confirms the application was accepted
failure_patterns.consequence: One day of work before EAD arrives constitutes unauthorized employment, terminating F-1 status with no reinstatement path and closing all routes to H-1B and green card
warning_signs: Student asks 'can I start Monday since USCIS approved my OPT?'
Student receives job offer with start date before EAD is expected to arrive and does not push back

failure_patterns.pattern: Confusing program completion date with graduation ceremony date when calculating the 60-day grace period
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Universities prominently advertise graduation ceremonies; the academic completion date buried in registrar records is less visible and emotionally less salient
failure_patterns.consequence: Student believes they have weeks or months more than they actually do; accrues unlawful presence without realizing it, triggering 3-year or 10-year bars on re-entry
warning_signs: Student references graduation ceremony date when asked about their completion date
Student has not confirmed completion date with registrar or DSO

failure_patterns.pattern: Failing to report address changes within the required window
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students treat address reporting as administrative paperwork rather than a legal obligation; the consequence feels distant and the action feels low-priority during a move
failure_patterns.consequence: SEVIS records a compliance failure that surfaces during H-1B or OPT processing as an RFE or denial; even a single unreported move can delay H-1B approval by 3–4 months and require attorney intervention
warning_signs: Student moved in the past year but cannot recall notifying DSO
DSO address on file does not match current residence
Student unaware of the 15-day (F-1) or 10-day (J-1) reporting deadline

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating immigration as a problem to solve when urgent rather than a system to navigate continuously
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Immigration feels abstract until a deadline is imminent; students are focused on academics and job search and defer immigration planning until it becomes a crisis
failure_patterns.consequence: Options that required early action (correct program enrollment, track record building, employer selection) are no longer available when the deadline arrives
warning_signs: Student cannot name their OPT end date or CRS score
Student has never researched the visa category they need to transition to next
Student's plan for the next status transition is 'I'll figure it out when I get there'

failure_patterns.pattern: India-born applicants file EB-2 without researching their actual priority date wait time, only discovering the 50-100 year backlog years into the process
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students focus on whether they qualify (education, experience) rather than whether the visa will actually be available to them within a career-relevant timeframe — qualifications feel controllable, bureaucratic backlogs feel abstract
failure_patterns.consequence: Years spent in H-1B dependency, constrained job mobility, and deferred life decisions based on a pathway that may never materialize under current law
warning_signs: Student asks 'do I qualify for EB-2?' without also asking 'what is my priority date wait time?'
Student from India or China is planning to file EB-2 but has never looked at the Visa Bulletin

failure_patterns.pattern: Employer submits duplicate H-1B registrations for the same beneficiary, resulting in automatic denial
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Employer has multiple internal teams or staffing agencies each submitting independently, or attempts to game the system with multiple entries
failure_patterns.consequence: All registrations for that beneficiary are invalidated — the student gets zero entries in the lottery for that year
warning_signs: Student is working with a staffing agency AND a direct employer simultaneously
Employer has decentralized HR with multiple immigration vendors

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating H-1B selection as a near-certainty and making long-term life plans dependent on it
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students anchor on the process (employer files, student registers) and confuse process completion with outcome certainty; historical approval stories from peers reinforce this bias.
failure_patterns.consequence: No backup plan exists when lottery selection fails; student faces abrupt status loss, job termination, or forced departure with no runway for alternatives.
warning_signs: Student cannot name a single alternative if H-1B is not selected
Student has signed a lease or made financial commitments contingent on H-1B approval
Student has not asked employer about cap-exempt options or prior sponsorship history

failure_patterns.pattern: Applying for OPT fewer than 90 days before graduation, resulting in a work authorization gap
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students focus on academic completion and job searching, underestimating USCIS processing times and the complexity of the DSO recommendation step
failure_patterns.consequence: EAD card arrives after graduation and sometimes after a job start date, forcing the employer to delay onboarding or rescind the offer; every day without an EAD counts as an unemployment day
warning_signs: Student has not contacted DSO about OPT until after receiving a job offer
Student believes OPT starts automatically at graduation without a separate application

failure_patterns.pattern: J-1 student allowing health insurance to lapse, assuming it can be renewed retroactively
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students treat health insurance as optional or assume a brief gap is tolerable. J-1 regulations have zero tolerance for willful non-compliance.
failure_patterns.consequence: RO is required to terminate J-1 status in SEVIS. Terminated J-1 students cannot reinstate. They must depart the US.
warning_signs: Student asks about switching insurance plans and mentions a gap in coverage
Student reports financial hardship and asks what they can cut from their budget

failure_patterns.pattern: Selecting 'Yes' to the job offer question during the IRCC questionnaire, resulting in a closed work permit application instead of an open PGWP
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The questionnaire wording is counterintuitive — students with jobs think honesty requires them to say yes, not understanding that 'yes' routes to an employer-specific permit
failure_patterns.consequence: Student receives a closed work permit tied to one employer instead of an open PGWP; loses the flexibility to change jobs freely and may need to restart the application process
warning_signs: Student has already accepted a job offer before applying for PGWP
Student did not use an RCIC or consult the school's step-by-step guide before starting the questionnaire

failure_patterns.pattern: Working in a TEER 4/5 job for 12+ months believing it counts toward CEC
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students are unaware of TEER classifications or assume any skilled work counts; employers do not flag immigration implications of job classification
failure_patterns.consequence: An entire year of PGWP validity is consumed with no CEC-eligible hours accumulated; the student must restart the 1,560-hour clock with less PGWP time remaining
warning_signs: Student has not looked up their job's NOC code
Student is working in retail, food service, or manual trades and has not confirmed TEER level

failure_patterns.pattern: Using a pre-March 2025 CRS calculator that still includes job offer points
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Many third-party CRS calculators and immigration guides were not updated promptly after the March 25, 2025 rule change; students use outdated tools and receive inflated scores
failure_patterns.consequence: Student believes their CRS score is competitive, delays improving other factors, and then discovers their actual score is 50–200 points lower when they submit a profile — potentially missing draw cycles during PGWP validity
warning_signs: Student cites a CRS score that includes 50 or 200 points for 'arranged employment'
Student's primary immigration strategy is 'get a job offer to boost my CRS'
Student has not checked IRCC's official CRS tool since early 2025

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating 'PGWP-eligible institution' as equivalent to 'PGWP-eligible program'
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Before November 2024, attending an eligible institution was sufficient — the field of study did not matter. Students and even some advisors carry over this outdated mental model post-reform
failure_patterns.consequence: A student at a designated learning institution (DLI) in an ineligible field completes their program and cannot obtain a PGWP — they are left without legal work authorization and cannot build CEC work experience
warning_signs: Student says 'my school is PGWP-eligible' when asked about their PGWP qualification
Student has not verified their specific program's CIP code against the current IRCC list

failure_patterns.pattern: Accumulating 1–2 years of TEER 4–5 work experience and then discovering it doesn't count for CEC
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students are unaware of NOC TEER categories and assume any legal Canadian work experience counts toward CEC eligibility; they prioritize income or convenience over immigration strategy
failure_patterns.consequence: The student must find a TEER 0–3 job and work another full year before becoming CEC-eligible, often while their PGWP approaches expiry and age penalty increases
warning_signs: Working in retail, food service, warehousing, or manual labor on PGWP
Never having looked up the NOC code of your current job
Assuming 'Canadian work experience' means any work done in Canada

failure_patterns.pattern: Accepting a job in a province with no active tech PNP stream and assuming Express Entry alone will be sufficient
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Job offers feel urgent and concrete; immigration strategy feels abstract — students optimize for employment without modeling downstream immigration implications
failure_patterns.consequence: Candidate is locked into a province with no PNP pathway and must rely solely on CRS score which may not reach ITA threshold
warning_signs: Student accepted job in province with no tech PNP stream and has CRS score below 490
Student has never checked whether their employer's province has a targeted tech PNP stream

failure_patterns.pattern: Medical emergency causes enrollment drop without DSO involvement, and student waits until health improves to seek help
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students in crisis prioritize health over administrative process — understandably — but are unaware that the clock for status violation and the 5-month reinstatement window starts immediately
failure_patterns.consequence: By the time the student seeks DSO help, weeks or months of out-of-status time have accrued, potentially pushing them past the 5-month window for standard reinstatement
warning_signs: Student mentions a health crisis that coincided with a semester where they dropped or stopped attending courses
Student is unsure exactly when they fell below full-time enrollment
Student has not spoken to DSO since the health event began

failure_patterns.pattern: Accumulating 12+ months of full-time CPT without realizing OPT is being permanently forfeited
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Employers and program advisors describe CPT as 'unlimited' (which is true for CPT itself) without disclosing the separate OPT eligibility rule; students optimize for current income without understanding the long-term cost
failure_patterns.consequence: Permanent, irreversible loss of OPT eligibility; student cannot bridge to H-1B through standard OPT pathway and must find alternative status immediately upon graduation
warning_signs: Student is approaching or has exceeded 11 months of full-time CPT and has not been warned by DSO
Student describes their internship as 'CPT, so it's fine' without knowing their cumulative total

failure_patterns.pattern: Applying for OPT on or after graduation day instead of 60–90 days before
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students treat OPT as something to do 'after' graduation; they underestimate USCIS processing time and overestimate how quickly they need authorization
failure_patterns.consequence: Multi-month work authorization gap; job offers rescinded or deferred; unemployment days accumulate before any work is possible
warning_signs: Student plans to 'celebrate graduation first then deal with OPT paperwork'
Student has not contacted DSO to initiate SEVIS authorization

failure_patterns.pattern: Working one day before EAD card arrives
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students rationalize that approved = authorized; the physical card feels like a formality when approval notice is already received
failure_patterns.consequence: Permanent unauthorized employment record in SEVIS; F-1 status termination; reinstatement typically unavailable for unauthorized employment; long-term H-1B and green card complications
warning_signs: Employer sets start date before student's EAD card has arrived
Student says 'my EAD was approved, I'm just waiting for the card'
Student unsure whether approval notice vs. physical card constitutes work authorization

failure_patterns.pattern: Accepting verbal or written sponsorship assurances without verifying execution capacity
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students are relieved to have an offer and interpret 'yes we sponsor' as a solved problem; asking probing follow-up questions feels awkward or untrusting
failure_patterns.consequence: Employer fails to file correctly, misses deadlines, or discovers they cannot actually sponsor — the student loses their work authorization and may have no legal recourse
warning_signs: Employer cannot name the immigration attorney or firm they use
HR cannot answer how many employees they have previously sponsored
Nobody internally has been assigned to track your status deadlines

failure_patterns.pattern: Assuming employer will manage the entire green card process and not tracking it personally
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Employees trust HR and immigration counsel to handle compliance — but attorney incentives are aligned with the employer, not the employee, and key deadlines are often missed
failure_patterns.consequence: Missing the H-1B 6-year cap extension window under AC21, failing to preserve priority dates, or losing portability rights through inaction
warning_signs: Employee cannot state their own priority date
Employee does not know whether their I-140 has been approved

failure_patterns.pattern: Missing the 14-day H-1B registration window due to delayed employer preparation
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Employers assume the window is longer than it is, or HR teams deprioritize visa administration until it's too late
failure_patterns.consequence: Student loses an entire fiscal year of H-1B eligibility and must remain on OPT or leave the US
warning_signs: Employer has not mentioned H-1B registration by early February
HR is unclear on what documentation they need from the student

failure_patterns.pattern: Assuming a STEM-sounding degree qualifies for STEM OPT without verifying the official DHS list
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students conflate departmental naming conventions or popular understanding of STEM with the specific statutory list; advisors sometimes also fail to flag this until it is too late.
failure_patterns.consequence: Student plans 36 months of OPT runway (12 + 24) but is only eligible for 12, losing 2 additional H-1B lottery attempts and 24 months of authorized employment.
warning_signs: Student has not personally looked up their exact degree program on the DHS STEM Designated Degree Program List
Student's degree is in a field adjacent to STEM (e.g., information systems, construction management, applied arts)

failure_patterns.pattern: Missing the 30-day I-765 filing window after DSO SEVIS recommendation, resulting in automatic denial
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students do not realize the 30-day clock starts from DSO's action in SEVIS, not from when they receive notification or gather documents
failure_patterns.consequence: OPT application is automatically denied; student must restart the process, losing weeks or months and potentially missing the post-completion filing deadline entirely
warning_signs: Student waiting to receive all supporting documents before initiating the I-765
Student unaware that DSO has already recommended OPT in SEVIS

failure_patterns.pattern: F-1 student accumulating 12+ months of full-time CPT without realizing OPT is forfeited
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: CPT is approved semester by semester without a cumulative tracker being shown to the student. Students focus on the immediate benefit of paid work, not the long-term cost.
failure_patterns.consequence: The student graduates with no OPT eligibility, leaving them with no legal work authorization and no time to transition to employer sponsorship.
warning_signs: Student asks about CPT for a third or fourth consecutive semester
Student has been on full-time CPT for more than 8 months

failure_patterns.pattern: Assuming any Canadian college program qualifies for PGWP without checking the CIP code (post-November 2024 cohort)
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students and even some advisors are unaware of the field-of-study eligibility change that took effect November 1, 2024; the rule only applies to newer study permit holders, creating a two-tier system that is easy to misapply
failure_patterns.consequence: PGWP denial, immediate loss of legal status, required departure from Canada, potential bar on future applications depending on actions taken post-denial
warning_signs: Student applied for study permit after November 2024 and is in a business, arts, or social science program
Student has not verified their program's CIP code against IRCC's eligible list

failure_patterns.pattern: Waiting passively in Express Entry pool with a low CRS score without pursuing PNP
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students believe the system is purely meritocratic and their score will eventually be reached; PNP requires proactive research many students don't undertake
failure_patterns.consequence: PGWP expires; student must maintain status through bridging permits or leave Canada; PR opportunity is lost or significantly delayed
warning_signs: Student's CRS score is 50+ points below the last 10 draw cutoffs
Student has been in the Express Entry pool for more than 6 months with no ITA

failure_patterns.pattern: Waiting in the general Express Entry pool indefinitely instead of pursuing PNP or category-based draws
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students assume the Express Entry pool is a single, uniform queue and that their profile will eventually be selected; they are unaware that alternative draw mechanisms exist with lower thresholds
failure_patterns.consequence: PGWP expires while waiting for an ITA that never comes; student loses legal status and must leave Canada or find another temporary status bridge
warning_signs: Student says 'I just submitted my profile and I'm waiting'
Student has not researched their province's PNP streams
Student's CRS score has been consistently below recent cut-offs for 6+ months with no action taken

failure_patterns.pattern: Delaying language testing until after graduation
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Language testing is a new requirement as of November 2024 — students who began their programs before this date may be unaware it exists; others deprioritize it because they feel confident in their English but have not formally tested
failure_patterns.consequence: Student graduates, attempts to apply for PGWP, and cannot submit because they lack a valid language test result — PGWP processing stalls, student status lapses, and work cannot begin legally
warning_signs: Student in their final semester has not yet booked an IELTS, CELPIP, or TEF test
Student believes their English proficiency from years of study exempts them from the formal test requirement

failure_patterns.pattern: Ignoring CRS score tracking until after graduation, then discovering disqualifying gaps
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students treat immigration as a post-graduation problem and assume the system will work out if they just get the degree; they experience optimism bias about meeting cut-offs
failure_patterns.consequence: Discovers late that their score is 30–50 points below the cut-off with no remaining high-impact levers — French takes time, age penalty has already begun, and the PGWP clock is running
warning_signs: Has never run the official CRS calculator
Believes Express Entry 'is just about getting a job in Canada first'
Doesn't know their own CLB score or language band results

failure_patterns.pattern: Applying to PNP with incorrect or mismatched NOC code
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students self-classify their NOC based on job title rather than actual duties, leading to applications under ineligible NOC codes or TEER levels
failure_patterns.consequence: Application refused; time lost; in some cases, misrepresentation flags can affect future applications
warning_signs: Student selected NOC code based on job title alone without reading the lead statement and main duties
Student's employer job offer letter does not reference a NOC code or uses a non-standard job title

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating summer break non-enrollment as a model for other semester breaks
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The summer vacation rule is a genuine exception that students overgeneralize — they reason that if they can skip summer, they can take a semester off in fall or spring with the same logic
failure_patterns.consequence: Student falls out of status during fall or spring non-enrollment; violation is not discovered until the next SEVIS check or until they apply for OPT/H-1B sponsorship
warning_signs: Student asks 'Can I take a semester off like I did over summer?'
Student is planning a gap semester in fall or spring for personal or financial reasons without DSO consultation

failure_patterns.pattern: Accepting a STEM field job offer without verifying employer E-Verify enrollment
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students assume large or tech-adjacent employers are automatically E-Verify enrolled; they do not verify before signing because asking feels awkward or they fear the offer being rescinded
failure_patterns.consequence: STEM OPT extension is ineligible to be filed with that employer, cutting maximum authorized work period from 36 months to 12 months and narrowing the H-1B lottery window
warning_signs: Student cannot confirm employer E-Verify enrollment from HR
Employer HR is unfamiliar with E-Verify when asked about STEM OPT extension requirements

failure_patterns.pattern: Failing to track cumulative unemployment days on OPT
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students mentally anchor to the 90-day number as large and safe; they do not account for compounding gaps — USCIS delays, job search time, onboarding gaps — that all draw from the same pool
failure_patterns.consequence: Automatic OPT termination and immediate out-of-status; no reinstatement mechanism; forced departure or status violation
warning_signs: Student cannot state how many unemployment days they have used when asked
Student is on second job search without having tracked days from first gap

failure_patterns.pattern: J-1 student letting health insurance lapse
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students who are young and healthy perceive health insurance as an unnecessary cost; insurance renewals happen on administrative cycles students don't track
failure_patterns.consequence: Willful health insurance failure terminates J-1 status permanently with no reinstatement path; student must depart the US immediately
warning_signs: J-1 student shopping for cheaper non-qualifying coverage
Student unaware that health insurance is federally mandated for J-1 (not just recommended)
Insurance expired between academic terms without renewal

failure_patterns.pattern: Using peer immigration advice as decision-grade input for high-stakes choices
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Peers are accessible, relatable, and share confidently; consulting an attorney feels expensive and intimidating; forum answers feel authoritative when they match what you want to hear
failure_patterns.consequence: Student makes a major decision based on advice calibrated to someone else's nationality, program, employer, and timing — and discovers the discrepancy only when the outcome diverges
warning_signs: Student's primary research source is a Reddit thread or WeChat group
Student is citing a specific friend's experience as the basis for their plan
Student cannot identify how their situation differs from the peer they are referencing

failure_patterns.pattern: Filing a NIW petition with weak evidence to avoid employer dependency, resulting in denial
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students overestimate the strength of their profile based on degree prestige rather than evidence quantity and quality; approval rate declining due to increased USCIS scrutiny
failure_patterns.consequence: Wasted filing fees ($715+), 4+ months of delay, and the need to pivot back to employer-sponsored PERM — losing months of priority date accrual
warning_signs: Applicant has fewer than 3 of the 6 NIW evidence types with substantial documentation
No attorney review of evidence package before filing
Applicant assumes PhD alone is sufficient for NIW approval

failure_patterns.pattern: Student registers at OEWS Level I because the job title is 'entry-level,' even though the salary qualifies for Level III
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Employer or attorney conflates job title seniority with OEWS wage level; Level is determined by salary amount, not title
failure_patterns.consequence: Student receives 1 lottery entry instead of 3, dramatically reducing selection probability
warning_signs: Employer says 'you're entry-level so you'll be at Level I' without checking the actual OEWS salary thresholds
Immigration attorney doesn't ask for the specific offered salary before assigning wage level

failure_patterns.pattern: Missing the OPT application window by filing too early or too late
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students are focused on job searching and graduation logistics; the 90-day pre-graduation and 60-day post-graduation boundaries are counterintuitive and easy to miscalculate.
failure_patterns.consequence: Permanent forfeiture of OPT authorization for that degree level; student cannot legally work in the US on F-1 status.
warning_signs: Student has not calendared both the earliest and latest filing dates
Student is relying on school DSO to remind them without confirming the actual dates independently

failure_patterns.pattern: Exceeding the 90-day unemployment limit without realizing it, causing automatic F-1 status termination
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students do not track unemployment days systematically; they assume searching actively for work means they are not 'unemployed' in the immigration sense
failure_patterns.consequence: F-1 status is automatically terminated; the student is out of status and may be barred from re-entry or future visa applications
warning_signs: No personal log of unemployment days exists
Student has experienced multiple short job gaps between positions

failure_patterns.pattern: Student fails to report to the DSO within 15 days of US entry, leaving I-94 unverified
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students are preoccupied with settling in and assume the arrival process is complete once they clear customs. The 15-day reporting requirement is not prominently emphasized.
failure_patterns.consequence: Failure to activate the SEVIS record and confirm I-94 status can result in auto-termination of the student's SEVIS record, effectively ending their legal status.
warning_signs: New student does not appear at the international student office before the first week of classes
Student mentions they never received an orientation email or letter after arrival

failure_patterns.pattern: Failing to upload language test scores because no dedicated field is visible in the IRCC portal
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The IRCC portal does not label a specific field 'Proof of Language Proficiency' — students search for it, do not find it, and either skip it or contact IRCC assuming it is not required
failure_patterns.consequence: Application refused for missing mandatory evidence; $255 fee lost; student must refile and loses work authorization time
warning_signs: Student says 'I couldn't find where to upload my IELTS scores'
Student submitted application without uploading any language document

failure_patterns.pattern: Failing to meet CLB minimum in one of the four language skills, causing Express Entry ineligibility
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students focus on overall language scores without checking all four individual skill bands; one weak skill below threshold invalidates the entire CEC application
failure_patterns.consequence: Express Entry profile cannot be submitted; student loses time and may need to retest, burning PGWP validity
warning_signs: Student knows their overall IELTS score but not their band-by-band CLB conversion
Student has a strong score in speaking/reading but suspects weakness in writing or listening

failure_patterns.pattern: Returning to home country after graduation instead of remaining in Canada to accumulate CEC-eligible experience
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students underestimate the CRS advantage of Canadian work experience and overestimate their ability to compete in FSWP from abroad with equivalent foreign credentials
failure_patterns.consequence: Student ends up in a far less competitive Express Entry pool (FSWP) competing globally, with structurally lower CRS scores and a much longer wait or no ITA at all
warning_signs: Student plans to return home 'temporarily' after graduation while job searching remotely
Student believes a foreign job offer in their field is equivalent to Canadian experience for immigration purposes
Student has not compared FSWP vs. CEC draw cut-off histories

failure_patterns.pattern: Self-filing PGWP with assumed or guessed CIP codes
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students who self-file immigration applications often copy CIP codes from peer accounts, forums, or institution websites without verifying against official IRCC classifications; they underestimate that CIP codes are a technical-legal element, not a formality
failure_patterns.consequence: IRCC flags incorrect CIP code as misrepresentation — application is refused and student receives a 5-year ban from Canadian immigration applications, ending any realistic PR pathway
warning_signs: Student is self-filing PGWP and sourced the CIP code from a Reddit post or peer
Student's institution registrar has not provided official documentation of the program's CIP code

failure_patterns.pattern: Dismissing French language investment and then being locked out of lower-cut-off draws
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Non-Francophone students perceive French as culturally irrelevant to their background or career, underestimate the CRS point value, and focus exclusively on English proficiency
failure_patterns.consequence: Competes only in general draws at 520+ CRS cut-off rather than French-language draws at 379–428; may wait years longer for an ITA or age out of peak scoring before receiving one
warning_signs: Has never researched French-language Express Entry draw cut-off history
Believes French is only useful for living in Quebec
Actively avoids French language coursework as 'not relevant to my degree'

failure_patterns.pattern: Assuming provincial nomination intention to settle is merely a formality
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students are told the obligation is 'not legally enforceable' and interpret this as permission to nominate from any province strategically with no follow-through
failure_patterns.consequence: Risk of program integrity issues; provinces may tighten criteria in response to abuse patterns; reputational and potential legal risk if misrepresentation in intention statement is later identified
warning_signs: Student explicitly states in application they plan to move to another province after receiving PR
Student has no employer, housing, or social ties in the nominating province

failure_patterns.pattern: Counting all enrolled credits — including multiple online courses — as satisfying the full-time requirement
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students see total credit count on their transcript and assume it satisfies F-1 requirements without understanding the in-person credit rule for online courses
failure_patterns.consequence: Student is technically out of status despite being 'enrolled full-time' by credit count — violation may not surface until OPT or H-1B application when SEVIS history is reviewed
warning_signs: Student's schedule shows more than one online or distance-learning course
Student enrolled primarily online due to work schedule, convenience, or COVID-era habits that continued
Student has never had the online course rule explained by their DSO

failure_patterns.pattern: J-1 student beginning on-campus work without obtaining written RO authorization, assuming it works like F-1
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: J-1 and F-1 students are often grouped together in orientation materials; the distinction in on-campus work rules is rarely emphasized, and students default to what they hear F-1 peers doing
failure_patterns.consequence: Unauthorized employment in violation of J-1 status, which can trigger status termination and bar future visa applications
warning_signs: J-1 student mentions starting a campus job 'like my F-1 friends did'
J-1 student has not contacted their RO before beginning on-campus work

failure_patterns.pattern: Traveling internationally during OPT without obtaining an updated I-20 travel signature
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students assume that if their visa stamp and EAD are valid, re-entry is guaranteed; the I-20 travel endorsement requirement is not widely understood and is easy to overlook during travel planning
failure_patterns.consequence: CBP denies re-entry at port of entry; student stranded abroad; possible OPT abandonment and need to restart immigration process
warning_signs: Student books international travel without scheduling a DSO appointment first
Student's last I-20 travel signature is more than 6 months old

failure_patterns.pattern: Assuming pre-graduation violations won't affect post-graduation immigration benefits
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students mentally compartmentalize their student years from their professional immigration pathway; graduation feels like a reset
failure_patterns.consequence: OPT denial, H-1B RFE or denial, green card complications triggered by violations from years earlier that were never resolved
warning_signs: Student graduated but never addressed a known compliance gap from earlier in their program
Student cannot recall full enrollment and address history during their degree
Student has never reviewed their SEVIS record

failure_patterns.pattern: Framing the stay-vs-return decision as a comparison of current living conditions rather than long-run compounding
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Current conditions are vivid and available; 10-year trajectories require deliberate analysis and emotional honesty about motivation; fear of return being perceived as failure distorts the comparison
failure_patterns.consequence: Student stays for emotionally driven reasons, takes on years of immigration uncertainty and constraint, and ultimately reaches a worse long-run outcome than if they had returned strategically
warning_signs: Student frames return home primarily as 'giving up' or 'failure'
Student has not done a purchasing power or savings rate comparison between markets
Student cannot articulate a specific career advantage from staying beyond 'opportunities are better here'

failure_patterns.pattern: India-born EB-2 holders never checking whether EB-3 downgrade would accelerate their green card
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The assumption that a higher preference category is always better is deeply intuitive but factually wrong when per-country retrogression affects EB-2 and EB-3 differently
failure_patterns.consequence: Waiting years longer than necessary when a legal downgrade strategy would have yielded permanent residence sooner
warning_signs: India-born EB-2 holder has never compared their EB-2 and EB-3 India priority dates on the Visa Bulletin
Immigration attorney has not raised the EB-2 to EB-3 downgrade option in consultations

failure_patterns.pattern: Student waits for H-1B lottery result before researching alternative visa options
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Optimism bias — students assume they will be selected and don't prepare contingencies until a denial forces them to act urgently
failure_patterns.consequence: By the time 'Not Selected' is confirmed (late March), there is insufficient time to build a strong O-1 case or find a cap-exempt employer before OPT expires
warning_signs: Student has never researched O-1 criteria or cap-exempt employers
Student's OPT expiration is within 6 months of expected lottery result

failure_patterns.pattern: Dismissing O-1 visa as only for celebrities or elite scientists without conducting a proper eligibility assessment
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students anchor on the 'extraordinary ability' label and assume it requires global fame; they are unaware that a structured set of documentary criteria applies and that academic achievements often satisfy multiple criteria.
failure_patterns.consequence: A viable year-round, cap-exempt pathway is never pursued; student accepts lottery risk or departure when O-1 might have been approvable.
warning_signs: Student with peer-reviewed publications, awards, or above-market salary has never discussed O-1 with an attorney
Student rules out O-1 based solely on its name or perception rather than a documented criteria assessment

failure_patterns.pattern: Starting STEM OPT work at a non-E-Verify employer
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Student assumes E-Verify is a formality or that the employer will enroll once the student joins; employer HR is unaware of the requirement
failure_patterns.consequence: Work performed without valid STEM OPT authorization; student is technically out of status and the employer faces E-Verify compliance violations
warning_signs: HR has not mentioned E-Verify during onboarding
Employer is a small startup or sole proprietorship with no prior international hires

failure_patterns.pattern: Student assumes academic transfer automatically handles immigration transfer
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students conflate academic transfer (registrar process) with immigration transfer (SEVIS release process). They believe being accepted to a new school is sufficient.
failure_patterns.consequence: The student's SEVIS record is not released to the new school, leaving them in a status limbo that can be treated as unauthorized presence.
warning_signs: Student mentions they 'transferred' to a new school but has not contacted the DSO
Student asks why their new school won't issue an I-20 when they were just accepted

failure_patterns.pattern: Traveling internationally after PGWP submission without a valid TRV or eTA, then being unable to re-enter Canada
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students know they submitted their application and assume 'pending status' allows normal international travel; they do not realize TRV/eTA renewal is impossible until PGWP is approved
failure_patterns.consequence: Student is stranded outside Canada, loses employment, potentially loses housing, and cannot return until PGWP is approved — a process that can take months
warning_signs: Student's TRV or eTA expires within the next 3-6 months
Student is planning international travel immediately after graduation

failure_patterns.pattern: Planning a 2–3 year post-graduation PR timeline on a PGWP that is shorter than that window
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students assume PGWP length equals program length without confirming; shorter diploma or certificate programs generate 1–2 year PGWPs, not 3-year ones
failure_patterns.consequence: Status expires mid-PR process; bridging open work permit may be available but adds complexity, stress, and uncertainty
warning_signs: Student completed a 1-year post-graduate certificate and is assuming a 3-year PGWP
Student has not confirmed their PGWP expiry date relative to their PR timeline

failure_patterns.pattern: Ignoring French language as a CRS strategy because the student is not a native French speaker
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Non-Francophone students assume French skills are irrelevant to their immigration path; they are unaware that CLB 7 French adds up to 50 CRS points even for non-native speakers
failure_patterns.consequence: Student misses one of the highest-yield improvable CRS factors; candidates near cut-off who could reach the threshold with French investment instead wait indefinitely
warning_signs: Student has any French language background (school, prior exposure) but has never tested
Student's CRS score is within 50 points of recent cut-offs
Student has never researched French-stream Express Entry draws

failure_patterns.pattern: Building an Express Entry strategy around job offer CRS points post-March 2025
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The rule that job offers added 50-200 CRS points was in effect for years and became deeply embedded in standard PR planning advice — many advisors and online resources still circulate this strategy without noting the March 2025 change
failure_patterns.consequence: Student accepts a suboptimal job or delays improving language scores and education credentials because they expect the job offer to boost their CRS score — score never improves as expected, and they miss invitation rounds
warning_signs: Student's Express Entry strategy explicitly relies on a job offer as a CRS points source
Student has deprioritized retaking IELTS for a higher score because they believe employer sponsorship will compensate

failure_patterns.pattern: Using pre-2025 immigration advice and expecting job offer points to boost CRS
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students rely on immigration blog posts, YouTube videos, or advisor guidance that was accurate before March 25, 2025 but is now outdated; the rule change is recent and not yet widely internalized
failure_patterns.consequence: Job search strategy optimized for CRS point gain yields no CRS benefit; student may have accepted a less suitable role believing it would accelerate PR
warning_signs: Advisor or resource mentions 'getting a job offer to boost your CRS' without noting the 2025 rule change
CRS strategy sheet lists job offer as a key lever
Candidate has never verified when their immigration information source was last updated

failure_patterns.pattern: Not maintaining an active Express Entry profile while pursuing PNP
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students focus exclusively on PNP application and let their Express Entry profile expire or go inactive, eliminating the enhanced nomination pathway
failure_patterns.consequence: If nominated, the +600 CRS points cannot be applied to a non-existent or expired Express Entry profile, forcing a base nomination which has a longer, separate PR process
warning_signs: Student's Express Entry profile has not been updated in 6+ months
Student is unaware of the difference between enhanced and base PNP nominations

failure_patterns.pattern: Allowing the 60-day grace period to expire without taking any qualifying action
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students believe the grace period extends their authorized stay rather than understanding it is only a window to take action — they treat it as a 60-day vacation
failure_patterns.consequence: Student begins accruing unlawful presence after the grace period ends; this can trigger 3-year or 10-year bars to re-entry if they subsequently depart the US
warning_signs: Student graduated more than 30 days ago and has not applied for OPT, transferred schools, or changed status
Student is 'figuring out next steps' without urgency after program completion
Student is unaware that the grace period has a hard end date

failure_patterns.pattern: Missing the 90-day employment start deadline on OPT by not tracking unemployment days from the EAD start date
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students believe the 90-day clock starts when they stop working or become unemployed, not from the EAD start date; they treat the clock as a 'gap between jobs' rule rather than a cumulative authorization rule
failure_patterns.consequence: Violation of OPT status conditions, potentially invalidating OPT authorization and jeopardizing any subsequent H-1B cap-gap or STEM extension
warning_signs: Student has not begun working within 60 days of OPT start date and has no imminent job start
Student is unaware of what date is printed as the start date on their EAD

failure_patterns.pattern: Assuming OPT transfers automatically to a new school when transferring SEVIS
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students conflate SEVIS record transfer (administrative) with OPT authorization (school-specific legal status); the distinction is not intuitively obvious
failure_patterns.consequence: Work authorization terminates the moment transfer release date is set; student is immediately unauthorized to work without recourse
warning_signs: Student says they plan to 'transfer their OPT' to the new school
Student initiates transfer in SEVIS without consulting DSO about OPT timing

failure_patterns.pattern: Concealing or underreporting status violations on immigration forms
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students fear that disclosure will harm their application and hope the record won't be found; they underestimate USCIS's access to SEVIS history
failure_patterns.consequence: Misrepresentation is a separate, more severe violation than the original status issue; can result in permanent inadmissibility, which is far worse than the original violation
warning_signs: Student says 'I'll just leave that blank' when asked about immigration history
Student unsure whether a past event counts as a violation and decides not to mention it
Discrepancy between student's narrative and what DSO records show

failure_patterns.pattern: Making program and employer choices without assessing downstream immigration pathway implications
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students optimize for program prestige, salary, or immediate opportunity without the knowledge that these choices determine which visa categories they can access years later
failure_patterns.consequence: Student discovers their program does not qualify for the extension they needed, or their employer cannot support the next visa stage, after the decision is irreversible
warning_signs: Student chose program based solely on rankings or cost without checking immigration eligibility criteria
Student accepted an offer without researching the employer's visa sponsorship history
Student cannot describe the full immigration pathway from their current status to permanent residence

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating the EB-2 pathway as a near-term plan when country of birth makes it a multi-decade commitment
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Cognitive optimism bias — applicants assume policy will change, that they are special cases, or that wait times will shorten — rather than planning based on current law
failure_patterns.consequence: Career and personal life decisions (job choices, financial planning, family timing, housing) made on false premises about timeline
warning_signs: Indian national says 'I'll have my green card in 5-7 years'
No contingency plan for Canada, return home, or alternative immigration pathways

failure_patterns.pattern: Form I-129 filed at the wrong USCIS service center after selection
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Employer files at a default location instead of the location specified in the selection notice
failure_patterns.consequence: Petition is rejected or significantly delayed, jeopardizing October 1 employment start date
warning_signs: Employer's immigration counsel does not reference the specific selection notice when preparing the filing
Employer reuses a prior-year filing template without updating the service center

failure_patterns.pattern: Accepting a J-1 position without checking the 2-year home residency requirement
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The J-1 is presented as a straightforward work authorization option; the home residency requirement is buried in program terms and not volunteered by all employers.
failure_patterns.consequence: Student becomes ineligible to change to H-1B or apply for a green card for 2 years without a waiver, potentially derailing entire long-term US residency strategy.
warning_signs: Student accepted a J-1 offer without consulting an immigration attorney
Student does not know whether their J-1 program category carries the 2-year requirement

failure_patterns.pattern: Submitting a vague Form I-983 that does not clearly connect job duties to the STEM degree
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students defer entirely to employer HR to draft the I-983; HR writes a generic job description rather than a degree-linked training narrative
failure_patterns.consequence: DHS audit finds the I-983 insufficient; STEM OPT authorization may be revoked and the student is left without valid work authorization mid-employment
warning_signs: I-983 uses phrases like 'general business skills' without naming specific courses or competencies
Supervisor has not read or meaningfully contributed to the I-983 content

failure_patterns.pattern: Student seeking reinstatement after being out of status for more than 5 months
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students are unaware of or underestimate the 5-month reinstatement deadline, often hoping the problem will resolve itself or waiting for the school year to end.
failure_patterns.consequence: Loss of reinstatement eligibility. The student may be required to depart the US and re-enter on a new I-20, if the school is willing to reissue.
warning_signs: Student mentions they stopped attending classes 'a few months ago' without notifying the DSO
Student asks if they can fix their status at the beginning of next semester

failure_patterns.pattern: Working full-time during PGWP processing without meeting all three eligibility criteria for 'work while you wait'
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students hear that they can work while waiting and assume the rule applies universally, without checking whether they violated work-hour limits during studies or had a part-time enrollment period
failure_patterns.consequence: Unauthorized work record on immigration file; potential inadmissibility finding; jeopardizes future permanent residence applications through Express Entry or PNP
warning_signs: Student worked more than 20 hours in any single week during the academic semester
Student had a semester of part-time enrollment
Student's study permit had a gap or expired briefly at any point

failure_patterns.pattern: Failing to meet the FSWP 67-point minimum grid score before submitting an Express Entry profile
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students conflate the FSWP 67-point grid (a separate eligibility threshold) with their CRS score; they submit profiles without verifying eligibility and are rejected or found ineligible upon ITA
failure_patterns.consequence: Application refusal after ITA issuance, wasting the 60-day ITA window and potentially losing Express Entry profile standing
warning_signs: Student has not separately calculated their FSWP 67-point grid score
Student is relying on CRS score alone as the measure of eligibility
Student has not obtained an ECA for foreign education credentials

failure_patterns.pattern: Not submitting spouse's language and education credentials for ECA/IELTS before filing Express Entry
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students don't realize spousal factors affect the principal applicant's CRS score; they assume only their own credentials matter and don't want to spend money on tests for a non-working spouse
failure_patterns.consequence: Files with a CRS score 10–40 points lower than achievable; may wait additional draw cycles or miss the cut-off entirely when a small boost would have secured an ITA
warning_signs: Married or common-law partner has never taken a Canadian-recognized language test
Foreign credentials of spouse have never been ECA-assessed
Candidate runs CRS calculator without entering spousal information

failure_patterns.pattern: DSO-triggered SEVIS termination due to unreported address change or missed registration
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students focus on employment and visa milestones while treating administrative compliance as low-priority bureaucracy; 15-day address reporting window is short and easily missed during moves
failure_patterns.consequence: DSO must terminate SEVIS record; student falls out of F-1 status; all downstream OPT and work authorization is voided
warning_signs: Student moved apartments and has not updated address with DSO within 15 days
Student deferred registration without formal DSO approval

failure_patterns.pattern: Delaying reinstatement action after discovering an F-1 status violation
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students hope the situation will resolve, fear the complexity of reinstatement, or are unaware of the hard 5-month deadline
failure_patterns.consequence: Every day of delay adds unlawful presence; past 5 months, USCIS reinstatement is unavailable; past 180 days, a 3-year re-entry bar activates; past 365 days, a 10-year bar activates
warning_signs: Student discovered a potential violation but has not yet contacted DSO or attorney
Student is 'waiting to see what happens' with a compliance gap
More than 2 months have passed since the violation began without action

failure_patterns.pattern: Not negotiating salary before H-1B filing under the wage-weighted lottery, leaving both income and lottery odds on the table
