junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: How they evaluate the US vs. Canada trade-off
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Compares salaries and picks the US because the numbers are higher; treats immigration uncertainty as a background risk rather than a primary variable
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Filters first by country of birth and GC backlog reality; runs expected-value calculation on probability-weighted salary vs. immigration certainty; treats the lottery as a structural constraint, not a luck problem
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Indian/Chinese nationals who choose US on salary alone may spend a decade earning more but end up with no PR in either country

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Hand the I-983 entirely to employer HR and assume they know what they are doing because it is an employer-submitted form
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Actively collaborate with HR, involve the DSO for review, and ensure the training plan contains specific project-level descriptions linking degree to job duties before submission
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: First-time STEM OPT employers routinely submit deficient I-983s; the student bears the consequences of the RFE delay, not HR

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: How to assess PGWP eligibility after a program change
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Focuses on the program name and whether it appears on the eligible list today; assumes that if the current program is not on the list, PGWP is gone
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: First checks enrollment date against grandfathering cutoff; only then evaluates program-list status; understands that enrollment date, not program-change date, is the controlling variable
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Misapplying the analysis leads to unnecessary panic and potentially incorrect decisions (e.g., scrambling to switch back to CS when grandfathering already provides protection)

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Planning horizon for H-1B sponsorship conversations
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Raises H-1B topic with employer only when STEM OPT is nearing expiration or after lottery registration opens
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Initiates H-1B sponsorship conversation during OPT year one, well before any deadline, treating it as a career planning discussion rather than a visa emergency
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Employer internal approval processes — legal review, budget allocation, policy vetting — can take months; early conversations create runway that last-minute conversations cannot
junior_vs_senior_signals._source: zhihu_F_1留学生从OPT到H_1B_中间有哪些需要注意的关键节点.txt

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: How to think about H-1B lottery failure
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Treat three lottery misses as the end of US career prospects; frame the question as 'what are my options now that everything has failed'
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Treat the lottery as one mechanism among several; begin building O-1/NIW evidence and evaluating Canada in parallel during OPT years so that lottery failure triggers a prepared transition rather than a crisis
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Waiting until after three misses compresses multi-year preparation requirements into a short crisis window, forcing worse outcomes
junior_vs_senior_signals._source: zhihu_H_1B三次抽签都没中的话_留在美国还有哪些合法途径.txt

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Response to a low CRS score
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Monitors draw cutoffs anxiously, waits for a favorable round, assumes the score will eventually be drawn
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Immediately maps which factors are controllable (French, spousal language, work experience accumulation timeline), which require external selection (PNP), and builds a multi-year action plan
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Passive waiting while age-based points erode and PNP windows open and close is a high-cost mistake; proactive multi-lever planning compounds advantages
junior_vs_senior_signals._source: zhihu_加拿大Express_Entry的CRS分数怎么计算_有哪些可以主动提升的因素.txt

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: 对移民路径的认知框架
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: 将移民视为'读完书自然会发生的事'，关注'几年能拿PR'的时间线问题
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: 将移民视为CRS分数管理问题，主动识别法语、Job Offer、配偶加分等杠杆，并根据当前分数决定走联邦EE还是PNP
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: 被动等待者可能在PGWP到期前仍未收到ITA；主动管理者可将时间线压缩至18-26个月
junior_vs_senior_signals._source: zhihu_在加拿大读完研究生_毕业后移民有多现实_从PGWP到PR需要几年.txt

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: How to think about parental reunion when choosing a country
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Assumes this is a problem to solve 'later' after immigration status is secured; treats it as a procedural step that will be available when needed
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Models the parental reunion timeline forward from today — calculates parents' ages at each milestone (green card, citizenship, sponsorship completion) and evaluates whether the total timeline is acceptable before committing to a path
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: The 10–15 year US green card + naturalization + sponsorship pipeline can entirely miss the window for meaningful co-residence with aging parents
junior_vs_senior_signals._source: zhihu_在美国还是加拿大工作_对国内父母将来的团聚有多大影响.txt

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Framing the US vs. Canada decision
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Asks 'which path is more realistic?' as if one path objectively dominates — looks for a single correct answer applicable to all Chinese international students
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Immediately asks clarifying questions about the individual's priorities, field, family plans, and risk tolerance before offering a comparison — treats it as a personal optimization problem with multiple valid solutions
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: The naive framing leads to copying others' decisions without modeling personal circumstances; the informed framing produces a decision that actually fits the individual's constraints
junior_vs_senior_signals._source: zhihu_美国H_1B和加拿大PR_对于中国背景的留学生_哪条路更现实.txt

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: How they frame the green card timeline
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Asks 'how long does EB-2 take?' as if there is one answer; doesn't specify country of birth; assumes answer is 2-5 years based on colleagues from other countries
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Looks up the current Visa Bulletin immediately; calculates priority date gap for their specific birth country and EB category; models multiple scenarios including EB-1 alternatives
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: The naive framing leads to multi-year life planning built on a false timeline; the informed framing surfaces that the real wait may exceed a career lifetime and forces earlier strategic decisions
junior_vs_senior_signals._source: zhihu_美国绿卡排期对印度_中国大陆出生的申请人有多严重.txt

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Checks overall CRS cutoff scores after each draw and feels discouraged when they're above 520–530
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Tracks category-specific draw cutoffs by NOC group separately from all-program draw cutoffs, and calibrates expectations based on their specific category's historical range
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Category draw cutoffs can be 40–60 points lower than all-program cutoffs, completely changing the realistic timeline to ITA
junior_vs_senior_signals._source: canada_Express_Entry_CRS从450到490的真实经历_法语是关键.txt

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: PGWP duration expectations for non-standard program lengths
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Asks 'will my 1.5-year program give me 1 or 1.5 years of PGWP — does it round up?' — treats this as an obvious question with an obvious answer
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Looks up the specific IRCC policy on program length calculation; understands that PGWP duration mirrors program length without automatic rounding, and plans immigration pathway around the actual likely duration
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: A 6-month difference in PGWP duration can determine whether you have enough time to accumulate Canadian work experience for Express Entry or a PNP
junior_vs_senior_signals._source: canada_PGWP申请全流程_从毕业到拿到permit只用了5周.txt

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Who to consult before making structural changes
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Talks to academic supervisor and department advisor about switching programs
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Talks to international student advisor first to understand immigration constraints, then works with academic advisors to find solutions within those constraints
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: The order of consultation determines whether immigration constraints are treated as fixed parameters or discovered as afterthoughts post-decision
junior_vs_senior_signals._source: canada_PGWP项目切换的大坑_差点损失两年work_permit.txt

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Views H-1B transfer as a minor administrative step; assumes job changes are nearly as free as for a green card holder
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Recognizes the 60-day grace period as a structural negotiating disadvantage; factors this into salary negotiation, employer selection, and emergency fund planning
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Workers who underestimate H-1B dependency accept worse conditions, stay in bad jobs longer, and are more vulnerable during layoffs
junior_vs_senior_signals._source: canada_加拿大vs美国移民路线的真实对比_从两边都走过的角度.txt

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Spends most time on Indeed and LinkedIn Easy Apply because it is fast and feels productive
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Prioritizes internal referrals, direct company applications, and proactive hiring manager outreach; uses mass-apply platforms only as a supplementary volume filler
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Channel allocation is a primary driver of callback rate; misdirected effort produces high application volume with low yield and extended timelines
junior_vs_senior_signals._source: canada_在加拿大找第一份工作的经验_PGWP身份怎么向雇主解释.txt

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Apply for the lottery each year and wait for results before thinking about alternatives
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Maintain a parallel track: assess EB1A eligibility, Canada CRS score, and L1 eligibility at all times; treat lottery as one bet among several, not the primary plan
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Alternative paths require months to years of lead time; reactive planning after lottery failure wastes the preparation window

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Focuses on maximizing a single evidence type (e.g., publication count) and asks whether they have 'enough' papers
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Builds a diversified evidence portfolio across multiple categories (citations, peer review invitations, conference invitations, media, policy references) and maps each to a specific Dhanasar prong
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: USCIS evaluates totality of evidence across prongs; weakness in one category can be compensated by strength in others only if the petitioner deliberately constructs that argument
junior_vs_senior_signals._source: immigration_EB_2_NIW自己申请全攻略_I_140批准_分享经验.txt

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Avoids thinking about alternatives because it feels like admitting failure; has no concrete knowledge of Canadian immigration pathways or home-country career options despite multiple lottery failures
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Proactively researches alternatives in enough depth to form genuine preferences; negotiates contingency arrangements with employer before crisis; discovers that alternatives are 'genuinely acceptable options, not just backups'
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Under time pressure after a lottery failure, unprepared people make rushed high-stakes decisions; prepared people execute a plan they've already thought through
junior_vs_senior_signals._source: immigration_关于H_1B抽签后身份焦虑_说几句心里话.txt

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Resigns first to 'focus on the search' or to avoid awkward overlap with current employer
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Secures signed offer with confirmed start date before submitting resignation; minimizes the gap window to a deliberate, counted buffer
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Every day between jobs counts toward the cap; informed sequencing can be the difference between compliance and violation
junior_vs_senior_signals._source: us_visa_OPT期间换工作完整攻略_我踩过的所有坑.txt

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Optimizes own profile in isolation; never considers spouse language score as a variable
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Treats the couple as a joint profile; proactively has spouse tested and accounts for all spousal contribution factors
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Up to 20 CRS points are commonly left unclaimed by applicants who do not think of spousal factors as actionable

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Calculate your CRS score, submit a profile, and wait for an ITA — treat it as a queue
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Track draw cutoffs actively, identify whether your score is consistently clearing, explore PNP pathways if your federal score is borderline, and understand that timing of draws is an independent variable from score alone
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Passive waiting leads to missing draw windows or failing to act on PNP opportunities that could have accelerated PR significantly

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Treats returning home as an undifferentiated catastrophe; never examines it concretely; uses the vague fear as fuel for desperation
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Has done real research on the home-country scenario; can compare options with actual data; holds Canada preference without it becoming psychological dependence
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Desperation from unexamined Plan B fear leads to poor negotiating position, over-compliance with exploitative employers, and distorted decision-making across the board

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Treats maintained status as equivalent to the prior permit — assumes continued right to work during the processing gap
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Knows maintained status preserves legal presence only; plans for a potential work-gap period and avoids unauthorized employment
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Unauthorized work on a maintained-status gap can jeopardize future immigration applications including Express Entry

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Planning job start dates around EAD
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Uses the USCIS published processing time as a hard deadline and sets start date accordingly
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Treats the published time as a floor, builds in a multi-week buffer, and negotiates a flexible start date with employers that accounts for processing variance
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Processing variance is substantial (6 weeks to 6+ months documented in same period); planning to the average without buffer creates high risk of missing a committed start date

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: How to think about program length
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Think about program length in terms of time to graduation and tuition cost
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Think about program length as the primary driver of PGWP duration, which determines years of Canadian work experience, which determines PR eligibility speed through Express Entry
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: A 1-year difference in program length can mean a 2-year difference in PR-eligible work experience accumulation, fundamentally changing the immigration timeline

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Spends majority of search time on LinkedIn Easy Apply and job boards, treating networking as a secondary or uncomfortable add-on
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Treats local meetups, referral generation, and direct outreach as primary channels from week one; uses job boards only for targeting research
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Referral response rate is ~15x cold rate; misallocating time to low-yield channels wastes weeks of finite PGWP runway

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Emotional framing when disclosing visa status
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Apologizes, prefaces disclosure with expressions of worry, frames visa situation as a burden the company must accept
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: States status factually and neutrally as a logistics item, treats sponsorship inquiry as a standard alignment check rather than a confession
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Per high-upvote community signal (534 upvotes), the anxiety itself makes candidates come across worse than the actual visa situation warrants; tone change has direct impact on recruiter perception

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Immigration timeline awareness during job search
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Searches for jobs without factoring in H-1B cap season deadlines; discovers the timing constraint only after receiving an offer or being near OPT expiration
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Reverse-engineers the job search from the April cap registration deadline; knows offers must be secured and sponsorship confirmed well in advance of early March registration; communicates visa timeline proactively in interviews
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Missing the cap season by even a few weeks forces a full additional year of uncertainty or requires costly bridge strategies

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Treats each job gap independently; assumes a new job 'resets' the counter; focuses only on the EAD expiration date as the compliance horizon
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Tracks cumulative aggregate unemployment days from day one of OPT; knows the 90-day (or 150-day STEM) limit is total across the entire OPT period
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Naive tracking leads to inadvertent status violations discovered only after the fact, as confirmed by the 234-upvote real case in this thread

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Evaluating a job offer as a visa holder
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Evaluates offer on role fit, compensation, and whether employer 'will sponsor' — treats immigration compliance details as fine print to sort out later
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Treats E-Verify enrollment, cap-gap timing, and sponsorship track record as first-order screening criteria before evaluating role quality
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: An employer who cannot support STEM OPT or future H-1B forecloses years of authorized work regardless of how good the role is

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: When to stop worrying about a past immigration decision
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Treats each new immigration milestone (OPT grant, H-1B approval) as a clean slate that erases prior risk
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Understands that USCIS can reopen prior authorization questions at any petition stage; maintains documentation of all prior statuses and proactively briefs attorneys
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Complacency after initial approvals leaves people blindsided by RFEs years later with no documentation prepared

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Accepts employer's verbal assurance that they 'sponsor H-1B' without verifying prior filing history, law firm quality, or contingency planning
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Checks USCIS public H-1B employer data, asks for the name of the immigration attorney, confirms the employer has successfully petitioned before, and asks what happens if the petition is denied
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Small or inexperienced employers may fail the petition due to avoidable documentation errors, costing the employee their lottery slot

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Checks whether the employer has 'university' in the name or is listed on a university website, and assumes that confirms cap-exempt eligibility
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Asks specifically whether the entity's primary institutional purpose is research, whether it is nonprofit or government, and whether the applicant's role would involve genuine work for that entity — and confirms with an attorney
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Misidentifying an ineligible entity as cap-exempt wastes time and legal fees and may leave the applicant without a valid status path before OPT expires

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Treats March–May as a neutral waiting period and is caught off-guard by the intensity of the anxiety
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Identifies the waiting window as the peak stress period and proactively puts support structures in place before it begins — community, therapy, finalized backup plans
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Unmanaged anxiety during the waiting period degrades work performance, relationship quality, and decision-making at exactly the moment when good decisions matter most

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Response to first H-1B lottery loss
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Assumes it was bad luck, reapplies next year, makes no contingency plan, continues burning OPT time
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Treats first loss as a signal to immediately research and activate parallel pathways (Canada PGWP, other countries, employer-specific O-1 alternatives) while OPT buffer still exists
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: By the third lottery loss, STEM OPT may be nearly exhausted, leaving no time to execute a Canada pivot with adequate Canadian work experience for CRS scoring

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Response to EAD not arriving before expiry
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Stop working or panic when the physical EAD card does not arrive by the OPT expiry date
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Confirm timely-filed receipt notice is in hand, inform HR of the legal basis for continued employment, and continue working without interruption
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Unnecessary work stoppages create employment gaps and lost income that are entirely avoidable with knowledge of the timely-filed protection

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Treats PGWP as a reliable, single-track pathway; selects program for PGWP eligibility at enrollment and assumes the rules will hold through graduation
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Treats PGWP as one component of a multi-track strategy; simultaneously accumulates Canadian work experience via on-campus jobs or co-op during studies to hedge against policy changes
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Students who rely exclusively on PGWP are fully exposed to policy instability; those who accumulate Canadian work experience in parallel retain Express Entry eligibility even if PGWP rules tighten further

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Assumes each job gap is evaluated independently and believes 90 days resets with each new employer
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Maintains a running log of every unemployment day from OPT start date, treats the 90-day total as a finite, non-renewable resource
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Students who misunderstand the cumulative nature burn through their buffer unknowingly and trigger out-of-status situations
junior_vs_senior_signals._source: zhihu_F_1留学生从OPT到H_1B_中间有哪些需要注意的关键节点.txt

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Assume H-1B always requires lottery and that the only way around it is to get lucky
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Actively research whether your employer or a concurrent appointment qualifies for cap-exempt H-1B; use attorney-designed concurrent employment structures if needed
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Cap-exempt employment entirely removes the lottery variable, but most candidates are unaware it exists or how to access it
junior_vs_senior_signals._source: zhihu_H_1B三次抽签都没中的话_留在美国还有哪些合法途径.txt

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Treats PNP as a last resort to investigate only after multiple failed federal draws
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Researches PNP streams by NOC code and province from the beginning of the immigration planning process, positions career moves to maximize PNP eligibility in parallel
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: PNP streams have specific occupational, employer, and provincial residence requirements that take time to satisfy; late investigation means missed windows
junior_vs_senior_signals._source: zhihu_加拿大Express_Entry的CRS分数怎么计算_有哪些可以主动提升的因素.txt

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: 将PNP视为'失败者的备选方案'，优先追求联邦Express Entry
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: 将PNP视为CRS分数不足时的主动策略选择，了解各省分数要求差异，将特定省份的就业机会与PNP路径绑定规划
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: 僵持于联邦EE可能浪费PGWP窗口期；灵活运用PNP可实现更快、更确定的PR路径
junior_vs_senior_signals._source: zhihu_在加拿大读完研究生_毕业后移民有多现实_从PGWP到PR需要几年.txt

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Awareness of Super Visa as a near-term tool
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Only aware of permanent residence / immigration as a reunion mechanism; doesn't know Super Visa exists or understands it as a significant flexible option
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Uses Super Visa as the primary near-term parental co-residence strategy while pursuing long-term PR sponsorship as a parallel track
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Without Super Visa knowledge, Canadian-path immigrants may unnecessarily defer parental co-residence for years waiting for PR sponsorship lottery outcomes
junior_vs_senior_signals._source: zhihu_在美国还是加拿大工作_对国内父母将来的团聚有多大影响.txt

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Evaluating salary comparison between US and Canada
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Compares gross salary figures and concludes the US is straightforwardly better financially
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Adjusts for cost of living, taxes, family benefits (CCB, childcare), and life stage — recognizes the gap narrows or reverses depending on personal circumstances
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Decisions made on gross salary alone systematically underestimate Canada's total compensation value for people with families or in high cost-of-living US cities
junior_vs_senior_signals._source: zhihu_美国H_1B和加拿大PR_对于中国背景的留学生_哪条路更现实.txt

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: How they evaluate employer offers during green card processing
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Refuses all job changes out of fear of 'losing green card progress'; stays in suboptimal roles for years to protect perceived queue position
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Understands AC21 portability; knows which elements of the petition transfer and which reset; makes job change decisions based on actual legal risk, not general fear
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Naive pattern traps people in bad employment situations unnecessarily; informed pattern recovers career optionality without actually sacrificing more green card progress than necessary
junior_vs_senior_signals._source: zhihu_美国绿卡排期对印度_中国大陆出生的申请人有多严重.txt

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Assumes documentation (I-983, E-Verify) can be handled during the first weeks of work, like standard HR onboarding
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Confirms E-Verify enrollment and submits I-983 before the first day; treats this as a pre-condition of the start date, not a post-hire formality
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Working before documentation is complete may constitute unauthorized employment even with a cooperative employer
junior_vs_senior_signals._source: us_visa_OPT期间换工作完整攻略_我踩过的所有坑.txt

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Watches only all-program federal Express Entry draw cutoffs and benchmarks personal score against that single number
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Monitors category-based draws, PNP streams, and sector-specific rounds in parallel; understands that effective cutoff varies significantly by draw type and NOC
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Applicants waiting for an all-program draw may miss multiple lower-cutoff draws they would have qualified for in their specific category

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Application timing relative to the 180-day window
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Feels no urgency after graduation; may wait weeks or months before initiating the application
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Applies within days of receiving the official registrar letter, treating the 180-day window as a hard deadline with no buffer
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: IRCC has denied applications from people who miscalculated or procrastinated past the window — there is no appeal for a missed deadline

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Targets brand-name large companies first (Amazon, Google, Shopify) because they signal legitimacy and stability
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Targets mid-size companies (50–200 employees) in tech corridors first because applicant volume is lower and hiring managers are more accessible
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Large companies are application black holes for candidates without internal referrals; mid-size companies offer meaningfully higher conversion rates for first Canadian role

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: When to raise H-1B sponsorship need in the process
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Either raises it immediately in phone screen (too early, adds risk before company is invested) or waits until after offer (too late, feels like a bait-and-switch)
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Raises it after a technical round but before final round or offer — the company has demonstrated interest but no offer is committed, creating a natural alignment checkpoint
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Timing changes the power dynamic; raising it at the right moment means the company is already invested in the candidate and more likely to problem-solve rather than screen out

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Where to get authoritative OPT compliance guidance
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Relies on Reddit, peers, or the university international office for rules on OPT limits and grace period boundaries
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Treats community input as directional only; consults a licensed immigration attorney for any situation involving potential status violation or edge cases like freelance employment during OPT
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Community and institutional guidance is frequently incomplete or wrong on technical compliance questions; errors have multi-year immigration consequences

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Assumes the H-1B lottery is a single pool and trusts the employer's attorney to handle all registration details correctly
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Independently verifies that the registration was submitted under the master's cap pool and understands the probability difference between the two pools
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Missing the master's cap registration is a free improvement in odds that requires only awareness — failing to use it is a recoverable but unnecessary loss

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Thinks of Canada as a simpler but less desirable version of the US GC process; unaware of PGWP open work permit structure or PNP streams
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Knows that PGWP length is determined by program length (capped at 3 years), that PNP streams have different CRS thresholds by province, and that job flexibility under open work permit is a structural advantage over H-1B employer tethering
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Students who don't understand PGWP mechanics choose shorter programs that give them inadequate immigration runway

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Contact the DSO only when a problem has already occurred (e.g., after receiving an RFE)
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Engage the DSO proactively before filing — treat them as a free pre-submission review resource, especially for first-time employer situations
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Post-RFE remediation is harder, slower, and more stressful than pre-filing prevention; DSO involvement before submission catches common errors

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Contingency planning if H-1B is not selected
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Focuses entirely on winning the lottery; has no defined fallback plan
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Simultaneously evaluates cap-exempt employers, alternative visa categories (O-1, TN), further education for a new OPT cycle, and Canada as a backup jurisdiction before the first lottery attempt
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: With per-attempt odds of roughly 50-60% across three tries, students without contingencies face a crisis rather than a transition when they go unselected
junior_vs_senior_signals._source: zhihu_F_1留学生从OPT到H_1B_中间有哪些需要注意的关键节点.txt

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Understanding of the CRS scoring system
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Focuses only on the factors they already have (education, English language) and views the score as mostly fixed
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Systematically audits all controllable factors: French test, spousal language, overseas work experience documentation, job offer classification, and PNP as the ceiling-breaker
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Missing even one controllable factor (e.g., spousal language +20, French +50) can mean years of additional waiting
junior_vs_senior_signals._source: zhihu_加拿大Express_Entry的CRS分数怎么计算_有哪些可以主动提升的因素.txt

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Whether they consider Canada as a parallel strategy
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Treats Canada as a failure option — only considered if the US path fully collapses
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Models Canada Express Entry timeline and eligibility concurrently with US green card strategy from early career; treats it as a genuine alternative with different trade-offs, not a consolation prize
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Waiting until US options are exhausted means arriving at Canada PR evaluation from a position of desperation with less time, less flexibility, and potentially lower CRS scores due to age
junior_vs_senior_signals._source: zhihu_美国绿卡排期对印度_中国大陆出生的申请人有多严重.txt

community_misconceptions.misconception: TEF Canada and DELF are interchangeable for Express Entry — either one works the same way
community_misconceptions.why_believed: Both are French language tests and both appear in immigration discussions, making them seem equivalent in function
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: TEF Canada and TCF Canada are the designated French tests for Express Entry and Canadian immigration; DELF is a general French proficiency certificate not designed for immigration purposes and may not be accepted by IRCC for CRS claims
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Applicants who prepare for and take DELF instead of TEF Canada may find their score cannot be used to claim CRS bilingualism points, wasting study time and exam fees
community_misconceptions._source: canada_Express_Entry_CRS从450到490的真实经历_法语是关键.txt

community_misconceptions.misconception: Implied status during PGWP processing allows you to work full-time in Canada
community_misconceptions.why_believed: The phrase 'implied status' sounds like full legal status continuation; students hear 'you can stay' and assume all prior permissions including work authorization carry over
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: Implied status only preserves the right to remain physically in Canada. Work authorization during this period is limited to what the expiring study permit explicitly permitted (e.g., on-campus work or co-op if applicable). Full-time off-campus work is not permitted until the PGWP is approved.
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Graduates who start full-time jobs immediately after graduation under implied status are working without authorization, which can result in PGWP refusal and negative marks on future immigration applications
community_misconceptions._source: canada_PGWP申请全流程_从毕业到拿到permit只用了5周.txt

community_misconceptions.misconception: A 1-year Graduate Certificate is graduate-level coursework, so it should give roughly the same PGWP as a 2-year master's
community_misconceptions.why_believed: Academic equivalence feels like it should map to immigration equivalence — if the courses are at the same level, why would the government treat them differently?
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: PGWP duration is strictly tied to program length: programs under 2 years get a PGWP matching program duration; programs of 2+ years get the maximum 3-year PGWP
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: A student who switches from a 2-year master's to a 1-year Graduate Certificate loses 2 years of PGWP, potentially delaying PR by 2-3 years
community_misconceptions._source: canada_PGWP项目切换的大坑_差点损失两年work_permit.txt

community_misconceptions.misconception: You can get Canadian PR and then work in the US long-term without any risk to your PR status
community_misconceptions.why_believed: PR sounds permanent, and people conflate 'permanent resident' with 'citizenship-level freedom to be abroad'; the residency obligation is not widely understood
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: Canadian PR requires 730 days of physical presence in Canada within every 5-year rolling window; extended US work assignments can cause PR holders to fall out of compliance and lose their PR
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: PR can be lost or not renewed if residency obligation is not met; people who treat PR as a passive backup while maximizing US career time may find their Canadian status lapsed
community_misconceptions._source: canada_加拿大vs美国移民路线的真实对比_从两边都走过的角度.txt

community_misconceptions.misconception: If a company says they 'only hire permanent residents', there's nothing wrong with that and you should just accept it
community_misconceptions.why_believed: Employers citing citizenship or PR requirements seem to be exercising normal hiring discretion, and many job seekers assume this is legal standard practice
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: Discriminating against candidates based on immigration status or citizenship in employment may violate the Canadian Human Rights Act; this type of requirement is potentially illegal in many contexts
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Candidates accept discriminatory screening as normal, removing themselves from consideration without recognizing the legal dimension; also, companies may continue this practice unchallenged
community_misconceptions._source: canada_在加拿大找第一份工作的经验_PGWP身份怎么向雇主解释.txt

community_misconceptions.misconception: L1-B is a risky or unusual path that big companies only offer as a trap to get you to move abroad
community_misconceptions.why_believed: The requirement to work abroad for a year feels punitive; employees interpret it as the company offloading risk onto them
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: L1-B intracompany transfer for overseas employees is a well-established, legal H1B lottery bypass used routinely by large tech companies; the genuine risk is the 5-year US duration cap, not the path structure
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Candidates who reject L1-B based on this misconception may continue losing H1B lotteries indefinitely while a viable path was available

community_misconceptions.misconception: More recommendation letters is always better for NIW — 10 is excessive and will annoy the officer
community_misconceptions.why_believed: Some community members cited a belief that officers prefer concise packages and that letter quality declines with quantity; 6-8 is repeated as a folk rule
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: USCIS does not set a maximum; quality and independence matter more than count. Having 10 letters is not inherently harmful if they are substantive and non-redundant. The petitioner succeeded with 10
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Applicants may artificially limit strong letters of support based on an unverified community norm, weakening their evidentiary record
community_misconceptions._source: immigration_EB_2_NIW自己申请全攻略_I_140批准_分享经验.txt

community_misconceptions.misconception: Staying low-profile and not being too visible at work protects you as a visa holder because it keeps your options open and doesn't create complications
community_misconceptions.why_believed: There is a real logic that being dispensable makes it easier for a company to 'let you go' gracefully or figure out alternatives; it feels like reducing exposure to risk
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: Employer willingness to invest in visa sponsorship, explore alternatives, and support cross-border transfers is driven by perceived employee value; low visibility reduces that value signal and therefore reduces the employer's motivation to find solutions
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: The person weakens their own negotiating position for visa support while also reducing their quality of work life and career growth — a double loss
community_misconceptions._source: immigration_关于H_1B抽签后身份焦虑_说几句心里话.txt

community_misconceptions.misconception: Day 1 CPT is a gray area that many people use successfully, so the risk is overblown
community_misconceptions.why_believed: Survivorship bias: people who used Day 1 CPT without visible consequences are present and vocal in the community; people who faced enforcement or RFE complications are less likely to share publicly or are still in process
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: CPT requires that the work experience be an integral part of an established curriculum; Day 1 CPT from schools with no genuine academic program requirements fails this standard and is vulnerable to retroactive USCIS invalidation
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Retroactive finding of unlawful employment leading to unlawful presence, triggering 3- or 10-year bars to US entry; even a successful H-1B outcome can require months of delay and thousands in extra legal fees
community_misconceptions._source: us_visa_Day_1_CPT的真实风险_一个差点中招的故事.txt

community_misconceptions.misconception: Cap-exempt H-1B requires you to actually switch jobs and work full-time at a university or nonprofit — you can't keep your tech company job
community_misconceptions.why_believed: Most people understand H-1B as tied to a single employer; the concurrent employment provision is not widely known in the student community
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: USCIS allows concurrent H-1B employment: a cap-exempt employer sponsors the H-1B petition while the worker continues full-time at their primary (cap-subject) employer, as long as both employment relationships are genuine
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: People incorrectly dismiss the cap-exempt route because they believe it forces a full career change to academia, missing a viable path that preserves their existing job
community_misconceptions._source: us_visa_H_1B三连败后成功上岸cap_exempt经历分享.txt

community_misconceptions.misconception: If you've only been unemployed for less than 90 days in your current gap, you're fine
community_misconceptions.why_believed: The rule is often stated as '90 days maximum unemployment' without the word 'cumulative' being emphasized; each job gap feels like a fresh start
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: The 90-day limit is cumulative across the entire OPT period; all gaps since OPT authorization began are counted together
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Students who had earlier gaps may unknowingly be at or near the limit; exceeding it can retroactively affect H-1B petitions and future immigration applications
community_misconceptions._source: us_visa_OPT期间换工作完整攻略_我踩过的所有坑.txt

community_misconceptions.misconception: A job offer adds 200 points to your CRS score
community_misconceptions.why_believed: The 200-point figure appears frequently in older Express Entry guides and community posts; it circulates as a round memorable number
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: Job offer point values under Express Entry are 50 or 200 depending on NOC TEER level, draw type, and specific program stream; OP's TEER 0/1 offer added 50 points in a federal draw context; the conditions for 200 points are more restricted and poorly understood in the community
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Applicants may make career or relocation decisions expecting a 200-point boost and find their CRS increases by only 50, leaving them still below cutoff

community_misconceptions.misconception: If you're Indian and in tech in the US, the green card is hard but doable — it just takes time
community_misconceptions.why_believed: People see colleagues get H-1B extensions and assume the system is moving slowly toward resolution; the actual priority date data is not intuitive and the 40+ year figure sounds like an exaggeration
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: For EB-2 India, the current priority date is approximately 2012-2013 as of the post's writing. Someone entering the EB-2 queue today faces a wait that exceeds a normal working career under current quota structures
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Candidates remain in the US through multiple H-1B cycles, forgo Canadian PR eligibility windows, and eventually age out of competitive CRS scores — leaving them with neither US permanent residency nor a viable Canadian path

community_misconceptions.misconception: You have to stay at your current employer while on PGWP or you'll hurt your PR chances
community_misconceptions.why_believed: Many international workers arrive from employer-specific permit contexts (co-op, closed work permits) and carry that constraint forward; the fear of 'disrupting' a stable situation feels rational when your immigration status feels precarious
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: PGWP is an open work permit with no employer restriction; changing jobs does not affect Express Entry CRS score or eligibility
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Workers decline career-advancing opportunities unnecessarily, stagnate professionally, and accumulate resentment — all for a perceived immigration benefit that does not exist

community_misconceptions.misconception: You can work normally while your PGWP application is pending, because you're still on implied status from your study permit
community_misconceptions.why_believed: Implied/maintained status sounds like a continuation of the prior permit's conditions, and study permits allow some work — so the inference feels logical
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: Maintained status preserves the right to remain in Canada but does not extend work authorization; graduates cannot work until the PGWP is approved
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Working during the gap constitutes unauthorized work, which can result in permit refusals, bans, or Express Entry point deductions for misrepresentation

community_misconceptions.misconception: Premium processing is available for OPT I-765 applications if you need your EAD faster
community_misconceptions.why_believed: Premium processing exists for many other USCIS benefit types (H-1B, I-140, etc.) and applicants assume it applies broadly across all form types
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: Premium processing is not available for I-765 OPT applications; USCIS does not offer a fee-based expedite option for this category
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Applicants who plan around premium processing as a backup option will discover it does not exist, leaving them with no paid acceleration path if processing runs long

community_misconceptions.misconception: You can combine any programs you've completed to get a longer PGWP, as long as they're both at Canadian schools
community_misconceptions.why_believed: The combining rule exists and feels logical — more education should equal more PGWP — so students assume any combination counts
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: IRCC only combines program lengths for PGWP calculation if the programs are consecutive (no gap), both at designated learning institutions, and the combined length is accurate; a gap between programs may disqualify the combination
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Students plan their PR timeline assuming a 3-year PGWP from combined credentials but receive a shorter PGWP based only on the final program due to a gap

community_misconceptions.misconception: Mentioning future PR or sponsorship needs in a cover letter is honest and won't hurt you if you explain it well
community_misconceptions.why_believed: Candidates want to be transparent and assume employers appreciate honesty about long-term plans; they conflate transparency with strategic communication
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: Any mention of future sponsorship or visa uncertainty — even if inaccurate or framed positively — triggers employer avoidance; PGWP holders need zero sponsorship and must communicate that with zero ambiguity
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Silent rejection before qualifications are evaluated; the employer moves to candidates who appear to have zero immigration complexity

community_misconceptions.misconception: Being on F-1 OPT means you need employer visa sponsorship to work
community_misconceptions.why_believed: OPT is an immigration benefit associated with F-1 student visa, so students assume all their work authorization is employer-dependent; the word 'sponsorship' is used loosely to mean any immigration involvement
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: OPT is the student's own USCIS-issued work authorization (EAD card); the employer does not petition or sponsor anything for OPT — the student can work without any employer action
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Candidates incorrectly tell recruiters they need sponsorship when they do not, triggering early rejection screens that would not have been triggered otherwise

community_misconceptions.misconception: If a company verbally commits to H-1B sponsorship during your hiring process, they're legally obligated to follow through
community_misconceptions.why_believed: Employment offers feel like binding agreements once accepted; the hiring conversation feels like a contract moment; it is counterintuitive that such a significant term would be unenforceable
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: Verbal promises are not legally binding for H-1B sponsorship; promissory estoppel is theoretically possible but extremely difficult to prove and litigate in practice; only written documentation in an offer letter or contract creates meaningful recourse
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Employee spends 18 months building career assuming immigration continuity, then faces forced job search with depleted OPT runway and no legal recourse against the employer

community_misconceptions.misconception: The 60-day grace period starts when my OPT EAD card expires
community_misconceptions.why_believed: The EAD expiration date is the most visible date on the physical card; students anchor to it as the end of their authorized period
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: The 60-day grace period begins after the I-20 program end date, which may differ from the OPT EAD expiration date
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Students may miscalculate when their grace period starts and ends, potentially overstaying or misidentifying when they fall out of status

community_misconceptions.misconception: If you use more than 12 months of CPT, you lose OPT — so any CPT near that threshold is dangerous
community_misconceptions.why_believed: The 12-month rule is correctly remembered but the full-time qualifier is dropped; students aggregate all CPT (full-time and part-time) when assessing their risk
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: Only full-time CPT (20+ hours/week) counts toward the 12-month OPT-elimination threshold; part-time CPT has no cap
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Students with heavy part-time CPT history may unnecessarily forgo OPT-eligible CPT opportunities, or conversely, students with full-time CPT near the limit may not realize how close they actually are

community_misconceptions.misconception: USCIS hasn't banned Day 1 CPT, so it's legal and safe to use
community_misconceptions.why_believed: The absence of an explicit rule feels like implicit permission; students conflate 'not prohibited' with 'approved'
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: USCIS has not issued a formal ban, but enforcement is occurring through SEVP school terminations, consular officer flagging, and H-1B RFEs — legal gray zone does not mean low risk
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Students who rely on this reasoning may face H-1B RFEs, petition denials, or retroactive unlawful presence findings triggering 3-year or 10-year bars

community_misconceptions.misconception: If you have a US master's degree, you automatically get entered into the master's cap pool when your employer registers you for H-1B
community_misconceptions.why_believed: Candidates assume their employer's attorney handles this correctly by default and do not verify the registration details themselves
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: The master's cap pool registration must be explicitly selected during the H-1B electronic registration process — it is not automatic and requires the employer/attorney to affirmatively elect it
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Eligible candidates lose an additional free lottery entry, reducing their selection probability unnecessarily — confirmed by multiple commenters noting they have seen top-program graduates miss this

community_misconceptions.misconception: University hospitals are cap-exempt, so getting a job there solves the H-1B lottery problem
community_misconceptions.why_believed: University hospitals are affiliated with universities, and universities are definitively cap-exempt — the association feels transitive and automatic
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: University hospitals that primarily provide medical care do not qualify as cap-exempt even if university-affiliated; the primary purpose test is applied to the filing entity and sometimes at the department level
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Candidates accept positions at university hospitals expecting to bypass the lottery, only to discover the hospital entity does not qualify, leaving them in the same lottery situation with less OPT time remaining

community_misconceptions.misconception: Not getting selected in the H-1B lottery means you weren't qualified enough or didn't do something right
community_misconceptions.why_believed: High-achieving immigrants have succeeded through meritocratic systems their entire lives — the cognitive model is 'effort and qualification determine outcome,' which maps badly onto a random lottery
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: H-1B selection is a random lottery among registrants who meet eligibility requirements — qualification is a threshold, not a differentiator; selection is statistically independent of merit
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Internalizing lottery loss as personal failure causes shame, depression, and identity crisis that is disproportionate to the actual event and impairs recovery and decision-making

community_misconceptions.misconception: Losing the H-1B lottery twice just means you've been unlucky — try again next year
community_misconceptions.why_believed: Each lottery feels like a fresh chance; sunk cost of years in the US makes leaving feel like failure; peer pressure from others still in the lottery pool normalizes continued attempts
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: Each lottery is statistically independent; there is no streak correction. After 2-3 losses, STEM OPT years are substantially consumed and the window to pivot to Canada with sufficient Canadian work experience for CRS scoring is closing
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Person exhausts all OPT authorization, loses H-1B again, and is forced to leave the US with no PR anywhere — the worst outcome of all

community_misconceptions.misconception: You have to stop working the day your old EAD expires if the new one hasn't come in the mail yet
community_misconceptions.why_believed: The EAD card is the tangible document people associate with work authorization; its expiry date feels like an absolute cutoff, and employer HR often reinforces this by demanding to see a valid card
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: If the I-765 receipt notice was obtained before the old EAD's expiry date, the timely-filed rule permits continued employment — the combination of the expired EAD and receipt notice satisfies I-9 requirements
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Employees who follow this misconception stop working unnecessarily, lose income, and may create an employment gap — while employees who know the rule continue uninterrupted

community_misconceptions.misconception: If you changed your program to one not on IRCC's eligible list, you have lost your PGWP eligibility entirely
community_misconceptions.why_believed: The 2024 policy announcement emphasized program eligibility as a new requirement; students reading the announcement in isolation apply it without understanding the grandfathering clause that protects pre-November 2024 enrollees
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: Students enrolled before November 1, 2024 are grandfathered under the old policy; eligibility is determined by enrollment date, not the program at time of graduation or the date a program change occurred
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Students may unnecessarily switch programs back, extend their studies, abandon their pathway, or experience severe anxiety based on a misreading of their actual eligibility status

community_misconceptions.misconception: The 90-day OPT unemployment limit resets each time you start a new job
community_misconceptions.why_believed: The phrase '90-day limit' sounds like a per-episode allowance; people intuitively expect a fresh start with each new employer
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: The 90-day unemployment limit is a cumulative total across the entire OPT period; all gaps between jobs add together and count against a single shared total
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: A student who had a 60-day gap early in OPT, then faces a second gap, may believe they still have 90 days available — in reality they have only 30, and may go out of status without knowing it
community_misconceptions._source: zhihu_F_1留学生从OPT到H_1B_中间有哪些需要注意的关键节点.txt

community_misconceptions.misconception: H-1B is the only work visa that lets you stay long-term in the US after graduating
community_misconceptions.why_believed: H-1B is by far the most discussed visa in international student communities, creating a availability-bias illusion that it is the only path
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: O-1, TN (for Canadian/Mexican citizens), cap-exempt H-1B through qualifying employers, EB-1A, and EB-2 NIW are all legally viable long-term paths that do not depend on the H-1B lottery
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Candidates who believe H-1B is the only path do not build the evidence or connections needed for alternatives, leaving them with no prepared options after lottery failure
community_misconceptions._source: zhihu_H_1B三次抽签都没中的话_留在美国还有哪些合法途径.txt

community_misconceptions.misconception: If your CRS is below 450, there's nothing you can do except wait for the score to come up on its own
community_misconceptions.why_believed: CRS feels like a fixed score determined by credentials and experience already accumulated; the controllable levers (French, PNP, spousal language) are not prominently featured in most introductory guides
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: Multiple actionable levers exist: French language study can add +50 points, spousal language adds ~20 points, additional Canadian work experience adds incrementally each year, and PNP nomination adds 600 points — any one of which can change the outcome
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Candidates who believe the score is fixed take no action, age-based points decline annually, and PNP eligibility windows close while they wait
community_misconceptions._source: zhihu_加拿大Express_Entry的CRS分数怎么计算_有哪些可以主动提升的因素.txt

community_misconceptions.misconception: PGWP申请需要先找到雇主才能拿到，没有工作就不能申请
community_misconceptions.why_believed: 许多人将PGWP与H-1B、雇主担保工作许可混淆，认为工作签证都需要雇主支持
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: PGWP是开放工作许可，毕业后即可申请，与是否有工作无关，申请后可自由在任何雇主工作
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: 若误以为需要先找工作才能申请，可能延误申请时机，导致毕业后身份出现空档期
community_misconceptions._source: zhihu_在加拿大读完研究生_毕业后移民有多现实_从PGWP到PR需要几年.txt

community_misconceptions.misconception: Once I get my US green card, I can sponsor my parents and they'll be able to come within a year or two
community_misconceptions.why_believed: People know green card holders can sponsor parents; they conflate this with the fast IR-5 process available only to citizens, or underestimate preference category backlogs
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: Green card holders sponsoring parents use the F2B or family preference category, which has numeric annual caps and can have 5–10+ year backlogs; only US citizens get the unlimited IR-5 category (~1 year processing)
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: People build life plans around a parent reunion that is 15–20 years away, not 2–3 years; by the time it materializes parents may be too elderly or unwell to benefit
community_misconceptions._source: zhihu_在美国还是加拿大工作_对国内父母将来的团聚有多大影响.txt

community_misconceptions.misconception: PGWP holders don't face any hiring friction in Canada because they don't require employer sponsorship
community_misconceptions.why_believed: The legal reality is correct — PGWP is an open work permit requiring no employer action. The misconception extrapolates from legal fact to operational hiring reality without accounting for HR knowledge gaps
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: PGWP is legally frictionless but operationally misunderstood at scale — many Canadian employers conflate it with employer-specific permits and screen out candidates unnecessarily
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: PGWP holders who don't proactively explain their status lose job opportunities to candidates with PR/citizenship, even though they are legally equivalent for hiring purposes
community_misconceptions._source: zhihu_美国H_1B和加拿大PR_对于中国背景的留学生_哪条路更现实.txt

community_misconceptions.misconception: EB-2 India is slow but manageable — probably 10-15 years at most
community_misconceptions.why_believed: Early cohorts (pre-2010 filers) experienced shorter waits; people extrapolate from others' experiences without accounting for the exponential growth in India-born H-1B holders filing since 2010
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: EB-2 India priority date as of 2024 is approximately 2012. New applicants filing in 2024 with a 2024 priority date face estimated waits into the 2070s based on current queue depth and annual quota consumption rates
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Applicants plan US lives around a green card arrival date that will not occur within their working lifetime, leading to deferred major decisions (housing, family, elder care planning) that never get made
community_misconceptions._source: zhihu_美国绿卡排期对印度_中国大陆出生的申请人有多严重.txt

community_misconceptions.misconception: Your spouse must be physically present in Canada to have their language score count toward your CRS profile
community_misconceptions.why_believed: Many CRS factors require Canadian presence (Canadian work experience, Canadian education), so applicants generalize this to language scores as well
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: IRCC does not require a spouse to be in Canada to take an approved language test — scores from tests taken abroad are accepted for CRS purposes, provided the test itself is an approved test (IELTS, CELPIP, TEF Canada, TCF Canada)
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Applicants with spouses abroad delay or forgo adding spouse language scores, leaving meaningful CRS points unclaimed
community_misconceptions._source: canada_Express_Entry_CRS从450到490的真实经历_法语是关键.txt

community_misconceptions.misconception: The PGWP application deadline is tied to when your study permit expires, not when you graduate
community_misconceptions.why_believed: Students track permit expiry dates closely throughout their studies and conflate permit expiry with the PGWP eligibility window
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: The 180-day PGWP application window begins from the date of graduation (program completion), not the study permit expiry date. These dates can differ significantly.
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Students who wait thinking they have until their permit expires may miss the 180-day graduation-anchored window if their permit expires well after graduation
community_misconceptions._source: canada_PGWP申请全流程_从毕业到拿到permit只用了5周.txt

community_misconceptions.misconception: On H-1B, you must wait for the transfer to be approved before starting work at the new employer
community_misconceptions.why_believed: People conflate H-1B 'approval' with 'authorization to work'; the transfer process feels like it requires a new approval before any work begins
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: Under H-1B portability, a worker can generally begin working for the new employer once the H-1B transfer petition is filed (not approved), as long as the worker is maintaining valid status and the previous H-1B was approved
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Workers who wait for approval may unnecessarily miss job opportunities, lose income, or mismanage their 60-day grace period
community_misconceptions._source: canada_加拿大vs美国移民路线的真实对比_从两边都走过的角度.txt

community_misconceptions.misconception: Any 'work permit' holder requires the employer to file paperwork or provide sponsorship
community_misconceptions.why_believed: The term 'work permit' is associated in many HR professionals' minds with employer-specific or LMIA-required permits; PGWP is an open permit but the terminology is not commonly understood outside immigration specialists
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: PGWP is an open work permit — no employer involvement, LMIA, or sponsorship is required; the permit was issued by IRCC independently of any employer
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Employers screen out PGWP candidates unnecessarily, and candidates who do not proactively correct this misconception lose opportunities they were fully eligible for
community_misconceptions._source: canada_在加拿大找第一份工作的经验_PGWP身份怎么向雇主解释.txt

community_misconceptions.misconception: Once a lawyer is handling your immigration, you don't need to understand the strategy — that's what you're paying them for
community_misconceptions.why_believed: Professional services framing — you pay an expert so you don't have to think about it; this works for most professional services but not immigration strategy
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: Immigration lawyers are responsible for document compliance and legal filings, not for identifying your optimal multi-path strategy; strategic decisions must be made by the applicant
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Applicants who abdicate strategy to their lawyer may be on a default path (e.g., PERM EB2) when a faster path (EB1A self-petition) was available and never mentioned

community_misconceptions.misconception: Indian nationals cannot benefit from EB-2 NIW because the backlog is the same as PERM
community_misconceptions.why_believed: The severe India EB-2 backlog is well-known, leading some to conclude NIW offers no advantage; conflation of petition type with queue type
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: Indian nationals can and do file NIW; NIW allows filing without an employer sponsor, which has other benefits (portability, self-petition). The backlog is real but the petition category itself is not closed to Indians
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Eligible Indian researchers may not pursue NIW at all, losing the priority date and self-petition benefits even if the green card itself is distant
community_misconceptions._source: immigration_EB_2_NIW自己申请全攻略_I_140批准_分享经验.txt

community_misconceptions.misconception: Researching Plan B (Canada, going home) is equivalent to giving up on the US path and signals a lack of commitment
community_misconceptions.why_believed: Psychologically, considering alternatives feels like divided loyalty; culturally among high-achieving immigrant student communities, the US path is often framed as the primary goal and anything else as failure
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: Researching alternatives is standard risk management; it does not commit you to any path and empirically reduces anxiety, which improves decision-making on the primary path as well
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: People who avoid Plan B research are caught unprepared when the lottery fails, making rushed decisions under emotional duress with no prior information — worse outcomes on all paths
community_misconceptions._source: immigration_关于H_1B抽签后身份焦虑_说几句心里话.txt

community_misconceptions.misconception: Once you get an H-1B approved, any issues with your prior CPT are resolved and no longer matter
community_misconceptions.why_believed: H-1B approval feels like a clean slate and a signal that USCIS accepted your history; it creates a false sense that past authorization issues have been reviewed and cleared
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: H-1B approval adjudicates eligibility for that specific benefit at that point in time; it does not constitute a finding that all prior status and authorization was valid, and unlawful presence or employment issues remain available for scrutiny at later stages
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Applicants who believe they are 'safe' after H-1B approval may face serious complications during green card processing, consular visa stamping, or re-entry after international travel
community_misconceptions._source: us_visa_Day_1_CPT的真实风险_一个差点中招的故事.txt

community_misconceptions.misconception: I-983 for STEM OPT can be submitted after you start working, as long as the employer is E-Verify registered
community_misconceptions.why_believed: Employment paperwork (like I-9) is typically completed on or after the first day; students assume immigration documentation follows the same process
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: I-983 and E-Verify enrollment must be completed before the first day of work for STEM OPT to be valid
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Work performed before I-983 submission may be considered unauthorized employment, creating a status violation even if the employer later becomes fully compliant
community_misconceptions._source: us_visa_OPT期间换工作完整攻略_我踩过的所有坑.txt

community_misconceptions.misconception: French is too difficult or too slow to be a practical CRS strategy
community_misconceptions.why_believed: Most applicants from India or China associate French proficiency with years of study; DELF's reputation as rigorous reinforces this; applicants do not distinguish TEF Canada from other French tests
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: TEF Canada (not DELF) is the relevant test for Express Entry; experienced commenters (445 upvotes) describe it as more lenient and achievable at B1 level in 3–6 months for English speakers
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Applicants dismiss the highest single CRS lever available to them based on a misperception of difficulty, leaving ~50 points unclaimed

community_misconceptions.misconception: Express Entry is fully meritocratic and country-neutral — if your score is high enough, you will get PR
community_misconceptions.why_believed: The CRS system has no country-of-origin quota for PR (unlike the US EB system), which is genuinely true — but people conflate 'no country quota' with 'predictable, stable cutoff'
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: While there is no country-of-origin quota, draw cutoffs fluctuate between rounds — a 480 CRS score may receive an ITA in one draw and not in another, so score alone does not guarantee or time an invitation
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Candidates stop actively managing their profile and PNP options after hitting a score they believe is sufficient, then miss draws or find their score has become borderline as cutoffs shift

community_misconceptions.misconception: A 4-year bachelor's degree qualifies you for a 4-year PGWP
community_misconceptions.why_believed: The general rule is 'PGWP length = program length,' so a longer program intuitively seems to yield a longer permit
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: PGWP is capped at a maximum of 3 years regardless of program length
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Graduates who plan their PR strategy around 4 years of Canadian work experience via PGWP will find themselves short one year and need to adjust timelines or find bridge options

community_misconceptions.misconception: Your personal mailing address determines which service center processes your I-765
community_misconceptions.why_believed: Most government applications route by the filer's address; applicants naturally assume the same logic applies here
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: Service center assignment for OPT I-765 is based on the school's address, not the applicant's personal mailing address
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Applicants check processing time estimates at the wrong service center and have inaccurate expectations; may also cause confusion when trying to track or inquire about their case

community_misconceptions.misconception: Switching programs within the same university doesn't affect your PGWP
community_misconceptions.why_believed: It feels like an internal administrative change at the same institution, so students assume IRCC treats it the same way
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: PGWP length is based on the length of the program you actually complete; switching to a shorter program — even at the same institution — reduces PGWP eligibility to the length of the shorter completed program
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: A student switching from a 2-year Master's to a 1-year Graduate Certificate loses a year of PGWP work authorization, reducing Canadian work experience toward PR

community_misconceptions.misconception: Canadian employers know what a PGWP is and will understand you don't need sponsorship
community_misconceptions.why_believed: PGWP is a major federal program; candidates assume HR professionals at established companies are familiar with common work permit types
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: Many Canadian recruiters and hiring managers do not distinguish between permit types; 'work permit' generically maps to 'sponsorship required' in their mental model
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Candidates are filtered out without a conversation; the assumption of shared knowledge causes preventable rejections

community_misconceptions.misconception: Asking about future sponsorship needs in interviews is illegal national origin discrimination
community_misconceptions.why_believed: Students know employment discrimination based on national origin is illegal under Title VII and assume visa status questions are a proxy for national origin
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: Asking 'will you now or in the future require work authorization sponsorship?' is legally permissible; it pertains to employment authorization, not national origin; employers cannot discriminate based on citizenship status but can ask about authorization
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Candidates who believe this may refuse to answer or accuse employers of illegal conduct, damaging their candidacy and misunderstanding their actual legal protections

community_misconceptions.misconception: L-1 and O-1 visas are viable backup options for most international workers whose H-1B falls through
community_misconceptions.why_believed: These visa categories are frequently listed alongside H-1B in immigration discussions, creating the impression they are broadly accessible alternatives
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: L-1 requires 1+ years of employment abroad with a qualifying multinational company; O-1 requires extraordinary ability with high evidentiary bar — the vast majority of early-to-mid career professionals do not qualify for either
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Individuals over-rely on these alternatives in their contingency planning, only to discover ineligibility when they need them most, leaving them with fewer options and less time

community_misconceptions.misconception: I have 60 days after H-1B rejection to find a new job and start working
community_misconceptions.why_believed: The grace period sounds like a practical buffer and students assume 'authorized to stay' means 'authorized to work'
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: During the 60-day grace period you cannot work; you can only remain in the US to change status, transfer schools, or prepare to depart — new employment requires new work authorization obtained before starting
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Working during the grace period constitutes unauthorized employment, which carries serious long-term immigration bars

community_misconceptions.misconception: Cap-gap automatically protects you if your H-1B was filed for October 1 and your OPT runs out before then
community_misconceptions.why_believed: Cap-gap is discussed as if it is an automatic status bridge for all OPT holders in the H-1B lottery; the precondition requiring a filed petition before OPT expiry is routinely omitted
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: Cap-gap only applies if the H-1B petition was filed before the OPT EAD expiration date; students whose OPT expires before the April 1 filing window opens have no cap-gap protection
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Students fall out of status believing they are covered, creating unlawful presence that can trigger multi-year bars to re-entry

community_misconceptions.misconception: Getting OPT approved after a Day 1 CPT program means USCIS accepted your status history
community_misconceptions.why_believed: OPT approval feels like institutional validation of everything that came before it; students assume USCIS reviewed and blessed their full record
community_misconceptions.actual_rule: OPT approval is processed by USCIS as a discrete benefit request; it does not constitute a retroactive review or acceptance of prior CPT authorization
community_misconceptions.real_consequence: Students on OPT or even H-1B may still receive RFEs or denials in future petitions questioning the legitimacy of their original CPT, creating legal jeopardy they believed was closed
