failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Employees trust HR and immigration counsel to handle compliance — but attorney incentives are aligned with the employer, not the employee, and key deadlines are often missed
failure_patterns.consequence: Missing the H-1B 6-year cap extension window under AC21, failing to preserve priority dates, or losing portability rights through inaction
warning_signs: Employee cannot state their own priority date
Employee does not know whether their I-140 has been approved

failure_patterns.pattern: Missing the 14-day H-1B registration window due to delayed employer preparation
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Employers assume the window is longer than it is, or HR teams deprioritize visa administration until it's too late
failure_patterns.consequence: Student loses an entire fiscal year of H-1B eligibility and must remain on OPT or leave the US
warning_signs: Employer has not mentioned H-1B registration by early February
HR is unclear on what documentation they need from the student

failure_patterns.pattern: Assuming a STEM-sounding degree qualifies for STEM OPT without verifying the official DHS list
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students conflate departmental naming conventions or popular understanding of STEM with the specific statutory list; advisors sometimes also fail to flag this until it is too late.
failure_patterns.consequence: Student plans 36 months of OPT runway (12 + 24) but is only eligible for 12, losing 2 additional H-1B lottery attempts and 24 months of authorized employment.
warning_signs: Student has not personally looked up their exact degree program on the DHS STEM Designated Degree Program List
Student's degree is in a field adjacent to STEM (e.g., information systems, construction management, applied arts)

failure_patterns.pattern: Missing the 30-day I-765 filing window after DSO SEVIS recommendation, resulting in automatic denial
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students do not realize the 30-day clock starts from DSO's action in SEVIS, not from when they receive notification or gather documents
failure_patterns.consequence: OPT application is automatically denied; student must restart the process, losing weeks or months and potentially missing the post-completion filing deadline entirely
warning_signs: Student waiting to receive all supporting documents before initiating the I-765
Student unaware that DSO has already recommended OPT in SEVIS

failure_patterns.pattern: F-1 student accumulating 12+ months of full-time CPT without realizing OPT is forfeited
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: CPT is approved semester by semester without a cumulative tracker being shown to the student. Students focus on the immediate benefit of paid work, not the long-term cost.
failure_patterns.consequence: The student graduates with no OPT eligibility, leaving them with no legal work authorization and no time to transition to employer sponsorship.
warning_signs: Student asks about CPT for a third or fourth consecutive semester
Student has been on full-time CPT for more than 8 months

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating summer break non-enrollment as a model for other semester breaks
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The summer vacation rule is a genuine exception that students overgeneralize — they reason that if they can skip summer, they can take a semester off in fall or spring with the same logic
failure_patterns.consequence: Student falls out of status during fall or spring non-enrollment; violation is not discovered until the next SEVIS check or until they apply for OPT/H-1B sponsorship
warning_signs: Student asks 'Can I take a semester off like I did over summer?'
Student is planning a gap semester in fall or spring for personal or financial reasons without DSO consultation

failure_patterns.pattern: Accepting a STEM field job offer without verifying employer E-Verify enrollment
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students assume large or tech-adjacent employers are automatically E-Verify enrolled; they do not verify before signing because asking feels awkward or they fear the offer being rescinded
failure_patterns.consequence: STEM OPT extension is ineligible to be filed with that employer, cutting maximum authorized work period from 36 months to 12 months and narrowing the H-1B lottery window
warning_signs: Student cannot confirm employer E-Verify enrollment from HR
Employer HR is unfamiliar with E-Verify when asked about STEM OPT extension requirements

failure_patterns.pattern: Failing to track cumulative unemployment days on OPT
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students mentally anchor to the 90-day number as large and safe; they do not account for compounding gaps — USCIS delays, job search time, onboarding gaps — that all draw from the same pool
failure_patterns.consequence: Automatic OPT termination and immediate out-of-status; no reinstatement mechanism; forced departure or status violation
warning_signs: Student cannot state how many unemployment days they have used when asked
Student is on second job search without having tracked days from first gap

failure_patterns.pattern: J-1 student letting health insurance lapse
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students who are young and healthy perceive health insurance as an unnecessary cost; insurance renewals happen on administrative cycles students don't track
failure_patterns.consequence: Willful health insurance failure terminates J-1 status permanently with no reinstatement path; student must depart the US immediately
warning_signs: J-1 student shopping for cheaper non-qualifying coverage
Student unaware that health insurance is federally mandated for J-1 (not just recommended)
Insurance expired between academic terms without renewal

failure_patterns.pattern: Using peer immigration advice as decision-grade input for high-stakes choices
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Peers are accessible, relatable, and share confidently; consulting an attorney feels expensive and intimidating; forum answers feel authoritative when they match what you want to hear
failure_patterns.consequence: Student makes a major decision based on advice calibrated to someone else's nationality, program, employer, and timing — and discovers the discrepancy only when the outcome diverges
warning_signs: Student's primary research source is a Reddit thread or WeChat group
Student is citing a specific friend's experience as the basis for their plan
Student cannot identify how their situation differs from the peer they are referencing

failure_patterns.pattern: Filing a NIW petition with weak evidence to avoid employer dependency, resulting in denial
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students overestimate the strength of their profile based on degree prestige rather than evidence quantity and quality; approval rate declining due to increased USCIS scrutiny
failure_patterns.consequence: Wasted filing fees ($715+), 4+ months of delay, and the need to pivot back to employer-sponsored PERM — losing months of priority date accrual
warning_signs: Applicant has fewer than 3 of the 6 NIW evidence types with substantial documentation
No attorney review of evidence package before filing
Applicant assumes PhD alone is sufficient for NIW approval

failure_patterns.pattern: Student registers at OEWS Level I because the job title is 'entry-level,' even though the salary qualifies for Level III
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Employer or attorney conflates job title seniority with OEWS wage level; Level is determined by salary amount, not title
failure_patterns.consequence: Student receives 1 lottery entry instead of 3, dramatically reducing selection probability
warning_signs: Employer says 'you're entry-level so you'll be at Level I' without checking the actual OEWS salary thresholds
Immigration attorney doesn't ask for the specific offered salary before assigning wage level

failure_patterns.pattern: Missing the OPT application window by filing too early or too late
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students are focused on job searching and graduation logistics; the 90-day pre-graduation and 60-day post-graduation boundaries are counterintuitive and easy to miscalculate.
failure_patterns.consequence: Permanent forfeiture of OPT authorization for that degree level; student cannot legally work in the US on F-1 status.
warning_signs: Student has not calendared both the earliest and latest filing dates
Student is relying on school DSO to remind them without confirming the actual dates independently

failure_patterns.pattern: Exceeding the 90-day unemployment limit without realizing it, causing automatic F-1 status termination
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students do not track unemployment days systematically; they assume searching actively for work means they are not 'unemployed' in the immigration sense
failure_patterns.consequence: F-1 status is automatically terminated; the student is out of status and may be barred from re-entry or future visa applications
warning_signs: No personal log of unemployment days exists
Student has experienced multiple short job gaps between positions

failure_patterns.pattern: Student fails to report to the DSO within 15 days of US entry, leaving I-94 unverified
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students are preoccupied with settling in and assume the arrival process is complete once they clear customs. The 15-day reporting requirement is not prominently emphasized.
failure_patterns.consequence: Failure to activate the SEVIS record and confirm I-94 status can result in auto-termination of the student's SEVIS record, effectively ending their legal status.
warning_signs: New student does not appear at the international student office before the first week of classes
Student mentions they never received an orientation email or letter after arrival

failure_patterns.pattern: Counting all enrolled credits — including multiple online courses — as satisfying the full-time requirement
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students see total credit count on their transcript and assume it satisfies F-1 requirements without understanding the in-person credit rule for online courses
failure_patterns.consequence: Student is technically out of status despite being 'enrolled full-time' by credit count — violation may not surface until OPT or H-1B application when SEVIS history is reviewed
warning_signs: Student's schedule shows more than one online or distance-learning course
Student enrolled primarily online due to work schedule, convenience, or COVID-era habits that continued
Student has never had the online course rule explained by their DSO

failure_patterns.pattern: J-1 student beginning on-campus work without obtaining written RO authorization, assuming it works like F-1
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: J-1 and F-1 students are often grouped together in orientation materials; the distinction in on-campus work rules is rarely emphasized, and students default to what they hear F-1 peers doing
failure_patterns.consequence: Unauthorized employment in violation of J-1 status, which can trigger status termination and bar future visa applications
warning_signs: J-1 student mentions starting a campus job 'like my F-1 friends did'
J-1 student has not contacted their RO before beginning on-campus work

failure_patterns.pattern: Traveling internationally during OPT without obtaining an updated I-20 travel signature
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students assume that if their visa stamp and EAD are valid, re-entry is guaranteed; the I-20 travel endorsement requirement is not widely understood and is easy to overlook during travel planning
failure_patterns.consequence: CBP denies re-entry at port of entry; student stranded abroad; possible OPT abandonment and need to restart immigration process
warning_signs: Student books international travel without scheduling a DSO appointment first
Student's last I-20 travel signature is more than 6 months old

failure_patterns.pattern: Assuming pre-graduation violations won't affect post-graduation immigration benefits
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students mentally compartmentalize their student years from their professional immigration pathway; graduation feels like a reset
failure_patterns.consequence: OPT denial, H-1B RFE or denial, green card complications triggered by violations from years earlier that were never resolved
warning_signs: Student graduated but never addressed a known compliance gap from earlier in their program
Student cannot recall full enrollment and address history during their degree
Student has never reviewed their SEVIS record

failure_patterns.pattern: Framing the stay-vs-return decision as a comparison of current living conditions rather than long-run compounding
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Current conditions are vivid and available; 10-year trajectories require deliberate analysis and emotional honesty about motivation; fear of return being perceived as failure distorts the comparison
failure_patterns.consequence: Student stays for emotionally driven reasons, takes on years of immigration uncertainty and constraint, and ultimately reaches a worse long-run outcome than if they had returned strategically
warning_signs: Student frames return home primarily as 'giving up' or 'failure'
Student has not done a purchasing power or savings rate comparison between markets
Student cannot articulate a specific career advantage from staying beyond 'opportunities are better here'

failure_patterns.pattern: India-born EB-2 holders never checking whether EB-3 downgrade would accelerate their green card
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The assumption that a higher preference category is always better is deeply intuitive but factually wrong when per-country retrogression affects EB-2 and EB-3 differently
failure_patterns.consequence: Waiting years longer than necessary when a legal downgrade strategy would have yielded permanent residence sooner
warning_signs: India-born EB-2 holder has never compared their EB-2 and EB-3 India priority dates on the Visa Bulletin
Immigration attorney has not raised the EB-2 to EB-3 downgrade option in consultations

failure_patterns.pattern: Student waits for H-1B lottery result before researching alternative visa options
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Optimism bias — students assume they will be selected and don't prepare contingencies until a denial forces them to act urgently
failure_patterns.consequence: By the time 'Not Selected' is confirmed (late March), there is insufficient time to build a strong O-1 case or find a cap-exempt employer before OPT expires
warning_signs: Student has never researched O-1 criteria or cap-exempt employers
Student's OPT expiration is within 6 months of expected lottery result

failure_patterns.pattern: Dismissing O-1 visa as only for celebrities or elite scientists without conducting a proper eligibility assessment
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students anchor on the 'extraordinary ability' label and assume it requires global fame; they are unaware that a structured set of documentary criteria applies and that academic achievements often satisfy multiple criteria.
failure_patterns.consequence: A viable year-round, cap-exempt pathway is never pursued; student accepts lottery risk or departure when O-1 might have been approvable.
warning_signs: Student with peer-reviewed publications, awards, or above-market salary has never discussed O-1 with an attorney
Student rules out O-1 based solely on its name or perception rather than a documented criteria assessment

failure_patterns.pattern: Starting STEM OPT work at a non-E-Verify employer
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Student assumes E-Verify is a formality or that the employer will enroll once the student joins; employer HR is unaware of the requirement
failure_patterns.consequence: Work performed without valid STEM OPT authorization; student is technically out of status and the employer faces E-Verify compliance violations
warning_signs: HR has not mentioned E-Verify during onboarding
Employer is a small startup or sole proprietorship with no prior international hires

failure_patterns.pattern: Student assumes academic transfer automatically handles immigration transfer
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students conflate academic transfer (registrar process) with immigration transfer (SEVIS release process). They believe being accepted to a new school is sufficient.
failure_patterns.consequence: The student's SEVIS record is not released to the new school, leaving them in a status limbo that can be treated as unauthorized presence.
warning_signs: Student mentions they 'transferred' to a new school but has not contacted the DSO
Student asks why their new school won't issue an I-20 when they were just accepted

failure_patterns.pattern: Allowing the 60-day grace period to expire without taking any qualifying action
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students believe the grace period extends their authorized stay rather than understanding it is only a window to take action — they treat it as a 60-day vacation
failure_patterns.consequence: Student begins accruing unlawful presence after the grace period ends; this can trigger 3-year or 10-year bars to re-entry if they subsequently depart the US
warning_signs: Student graduated more than 30 days ago and has not applied for OPT, transferred schools, or changed status
Student is 'figuring out next steps' without urgency after program completion
Student is unaware that the grace period has a hard end date

failure_patterns.pattern: Missing the 90-day employment start deadline on OPT by not tracking unemployment days from the EAD start date
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students believe the 90-day clock starts when they stop working or become unemployed, not from the EAD start date; they treat the clock as a 'gap between jobs' rule rather than a cumulative authorization rule
failure_patterns.consequence: Violation of OPT status conditions, potentially invalidating OPT authorization and jeopardizing any subsequent H-1B cap-gap or STEM extension
warning_signs: Student has not begun working within 60 days of OPT start date and has no imminent job start
Student is unaware of what date is printed as the start date on their EAD

failure_patterns.pattern: Assuming OPT transfers automatically to a new school when transferring SEVIS
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students conflate SEVIS record transfer (administrative) with OPT authorization (school-specific legal status); the distinction is not intuitively obvious
failure_patterns.consequence: Work authorization terminates the moment transfer release date is set; student is immediately unauthorized to work without recourse
warning_signs: Student says they plan to 'transfer their OPT' to the new school
Student initiates transfer in SEVIS without consulting DSO about OPT timing

failure_patterns.pattern: Concealing or underreporting status violations on immigration forms
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students fear that disclosure will harm their application and hope the record won't be found; they underestimate USCIS's access to SEVIS history
failure_patterns.consequence: Misrepresentation is a separate, more severe violation than the original status issue; can result in permanent inadmissibility, which is far worse than the original violation
warning_signs: Student says 'I'll just leave that blank' when asked about immigration history
Student unsure whether a past event counts as a violation and decides not to mention it
Discrepancy between student's narrative and what DSO records show

failure_patterns.pattern: Making program and employer choices without assessing downstream immigration pathway implications
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students optimize for program prestige, salary, or immediate opportunity without the knowledge that these choices determine which visa categories they can access years later
failure_patterns.consequence: Student discovers their program does not qualify for the extension they needed, or their employer cannot support the next visa stage, after the decision is irreversible
warning_signs: Student chose program based solely on rankings or cost without checking immigration eligibility criteria
Student accepted an offer without researching the employer's visa sponsorship history
Student cannot describe the full immigration pathway from their current status to permanent residence

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating the EB-2 pathway as a near-term plan when country of birth makes it a multi-decade commitment
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Cognitive optimism bias — applicants assume policy will change, that they are special cases, or that wait times will shorten — rather than planning based on current law
failure_patterns.consequence: Career and personal life decisions (job choices, financial planning, family timing, housing) made on false premises about timeline
warning_signs: Indian national says 'I'll have my green card in 5-7 years'
No contingency plan for Canada, return home, or alternative immigration pathways

failure_patterns.pattern: Form I-129 filed at the wrong USCIS service center after selection
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Employer files at a default location instead of the location specified in the selection notice
failure_patterns.consequence: Petition is rejected or significantly delayed, jeopardizing October 1 employment start date
warning_signs: Employer's immigration counsel does not reference the specific selection notice when preparing the filing
Employer reuses a prior-year filing template without updating the service center

failure_patterns.pattern: Accepting a J-1 position without checking the 2-year home residency requirement
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The J-1 is presented as a straightforward work authorization option; the home residency requirement is buried in program terms and not volunteered by all employers.
failure_patterns.consequence: Student becomes ineligible to change to H-1B or apply for a green card for 2 years without a waiver, potentially derailing entire long-term US residency strategy.
warning_signs: Student accepted a J-1 offer without consulting an immigration attorney
Student does not know whether their J-1 program category carries the 2-year requirement

failure_patterns.pattern: Submitting a vague Form I-983 that does not clearly connect job duties to the STEM degree
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students defer entirely to employer HR to draft the I-983; HR writes a generic job description rather than a degree-linked training narrative
failure_patterns.consequence: DHS audit finds the I-983 insufficient; STEM OPT authorization may be revoked and the student is left without valid work authorization mid-employment
warning_signs: I-983 uses phrases like 'general business skills' without naming specific courses or competencies
Supervisor has not read or meaningfully contributed to the I-983 content

failure_patterns.pattern: Student seeking reinstatement after being out of status for more than 5 months
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students are unaware of or underestimate the 5-month reinstatement deadline, often hoping the problem will resolve itself or waiting for the school year to end.
failure_patterns.consequence: Loss of reinstatement eligibility. The student may be required to depart the US and re-enter on a new I-20, if the school is willing to reissue.
warning_signs: Student mentions they stopped attending classes 'a few months ago' without notifying the DSO
Student asks if they can fix their status at the beginning of next semester

failure_patterns.pattern: DSO-triggered SEVIS termination due to unreported address change or missed registration
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students focus on employment and visa milestones while treating administrative compliance as low-priority bureaucracy; 15-day address reporting window is short and easily missed during moves
failure_patterns.consequence: DSO must terminate SEVIS record; student falls out of F-1 status; all downstream OPT and work authorization is voided
warning_signs: Student moved apartments and has not updated address with DSO within 15 days
Student deferred registration without formal DSO approval

failure_patterns.pattern: Delaying reinstatement action after discovering an F-1 status violation
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students hope the situation will resolve, fear the complexity of reinstatement, or are unaware of the hard 5-month deadline
failure_patterns.consequence: Every day of delay adds unlawful presence; past 5 months, USCIS reinstatement is unavailable; past 180 days, a 3-year re-entry bar activates; past 365 days, a 10-year bar activates
warning_signs: Student discovered a potential violation but has not yet contacted DSO or attorney
Student is 'waiting to see what happens' with a compliance gap
More than 2 months have passed since the violation began without action

failure_patterns.pattern: Not negotiating salary before H-1B filing under the wage-weighted lottery, leaving both income and lottery odds on the table
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students treat salary negotiation and immigration strategy as separate processes managed by different parties; they fear that negotiating will signal they are difficult or jeopardize sponsorship.
failure_patterns.consequence: Lower wage level reduces lottery selection probability under the post-2025 system while simultaneously leaving real compensation on the table.
warning_signs: Student accepted the first offer without researching OEWS wage levels for the role
Student does not know which wage level their employer intends to file at

failure_patterns.pattern: Failing to report employer or address changes within 10 days, resulting in SEVIS violations
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students assume changes are administrative and low-priority; they are unaware the 10-day window is a legal compliance requirement, not a courtesy guideline
failure_patterns.consequence: SEVIS record is flagged for non-compliance; in serious cases DSO must terminate the SEVIS record, immediately ending F-1 status
warning_signs: Student changed jobs more than 10 days ago and has not contacted DSO
Student moved apartments and updated only the postal service, not SEVP Portal or DSO

failure_patterns.pattern: On-campus employee starts work more than 30 days before classes begin after arriving early
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students arrive 30 days early (as permitted) and assume that work authorization begins upon arrival, not upon the opening of the 30-day pre-class window.
failure_patterns.consequence: Unauthorized employment violation, which is a serious status breach that can result in status termination and bars to future immigration benefits.
warning_signs: HR or department asks DSO to verify student status for I-9 before the 30-day pre-class window opens
Student mentions they started working the week they arrived

failure_patterns.pattern: DSO fails to manually terminate non-enrolled students in SEVIS after the 30-day grace period, resulting in auto-termination
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: DSOs do not follow up on non-compliant students within the mandated 10-day and 21-day notice windows, leaving SEVIS records to auto-terminate.
failure_patterns.consequence: The school is out of compliance with 8 CFR 214.3(g), students lose status without notice, and the institution risks sanctions from DHS.
warning_signs: No structured process exists for identifying non-enrolled international students at the start of each semester
DSO has not sent the required 10-day non-compliance notice

failure_patterns.pattern: Assuming US green card is achievable within a reasonable timeline regardless of country of birth
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students research H-1B process carefully but often overlook or underweight the per-country green card backlog; the problem only becomes viscerally real years into the process
failure_patterns.consequence: Years or decades spent in H-1B limbo with no green card; career and life decisions constrained by employer dependency
warning_signs: Planning US career assuming green card in 5-10 years without checking priority date for your birth country
Not knowing your EB category's current priority date before starting a US job
failure_patterns._source: canada_加拿大vs美国移民路线的真实对比_从两边都走过的角度.txt

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating immigration lawyers as strategic advisors rather than execution agents
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: People assume that because lawyers are experts, they will proactively recommend the best path; in reality, most lawyers execute the path they are asked to execute
failure_patterns.consequence: Two people with identical profiles who ask different questions of the same lawyer end up on entirely different paths — one optimized, one default
warning_signs: You hired a lawyer but never asked 'what is my optimal path given my full profile?'
Your lawyer only ever discussed one visa category with you

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating the NIW Personal Statement as a CV or credential list rather than a policy-impact narrative
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Academic training conditions researchers to document achievements rather than argue for societal value; the distinction between listing and narrating is not intuitive
failure_patterns.consequence: Petition fails to persuasively address the Dhanasar prongs, leading to RFE or denial despite strong underlying credentials
warning_signs: Personal Statement organized by year or job title rather than by impact theme
Statement focuses on personal accomplishments without connecting to U.S. national interests or policy
failure_patterns._source: immigration_EB_2_NIW自己申请全攻略_I_140批准_分享经验.txt

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating uncertainty about future visa status as a reason to not fully invest in current life, relationships, or career
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Anticipatory loss aversion — the person pre-grieves a loss that hasn't happened yet, and withdraws investment to minimize the pain of a potential future departure
failure_patterns.consequence: Reduced quality of life during the waiting period, weaker professional relationships, lower career visibility — all of which reduce the probability of a good outcome regardless of whether the lottery succeeds
warning_signs: Saying 'I might have to leave anyway' when declining social invitations or career opportunities
Deliberately avoiding being seen as high-performing or essential at work
Feeling unable to make any long-term personal decisions (housing, relationships) between March and May each year
failure_patterns._source: immigration_关于H_1B抽签后身份焦虑_说几句心里话.txt

failure_patterns.pattern: Enrolling in a Day 1 CPT program based on peer referral without independent legal verification
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Social proof from friends who appear to have succeeded creates false safety; the risk is invisible in the short term and only materializes years later during downstream applications
failure_patterns.consequence: Potential retroactive unlawful presence finding, 3- or 10-year entry ban, H-1B RFE delays, and significant additional legal costs
warning_signs: A friend or acquaintance recommends the school and says 'lots of people do this'
The school itself is the primary or only source of assurance that the CPT is legal
No independent licensed immigration attorney has reviewed the specific program's CPT authorization
failure_patterns._source: us_visa_Day_1_CPT的真实风险_一个差点中招的故事.txt

failure_patterns.pattern: Passively waiting for H-1B lottery results each year without developing alternative visa pathways in parallel
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The lottery outcome is binary and outside the applicant's control, creating a psychological pattern of waiting and hoping rather than building backup options; each near-miss reinforces the belief that next year will succeed
failure_patterns.consequence: OPT and STEM OPT expire before an alternative is arranged, forcing departure or unauthorized presence
warning_signs: Applicant is on their second or third lottery attempt with no alternative plan
STEM OPT expiration is within 12 months and no cap-exempt or O-1 exploration has begun
failure_patterns._source: us_visa_H_1B三连败后成功上岸cap_exempt经历分享.txt

failure_patterns.pattern: Resigning first, then job searching — accumulating unemployment days over the 90-day limit
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students treat OPT unemployment limits like domestic employment norms, where taking time off between jobs is routine and consequence-free
failure_patterns.consequence: Original poster accumulated 95 days of unemployment, exceeding the limit; required DSO intervention; caused significant anxiety and immigration risk
warning_signs: Planning to 'take a break' before starting the job search
Not tracking cumulative unemployment days across all previous gaps in the OPT period
failure_patterns._source: us_visa_OPT期间换工作完整攻略_我踩过的所有坑.txt

failure_patterns.pattern: Comparing US vs. Canada career paths using salary alone, without factoring in the realistic green card timeline for one's country of origin
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Salary is concrete and easy to compare; the EB-2 India backlog is abstract and the multigenerational timeline feels unreal until you actually look it up
failure_patterns.consequence: Indian nationals stay in the US for H-1B cycles planning to eventually get a green card, spending 10-15 years in visa limbo before realizing the timeline makes the green card effectively unreachable within their working career
warning_signs: Saying 'I'll figure out the green card later' without checking current priority dates
Comparing Bay Area vs. Toronto salaries without mentioning EB-2 India wait times in the same analysis

failure_patterns.pattern: Submitting multiple USCIS contact center inquiries and assuming that is sufficient escalation
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The USCIS website presents the contact center as the official escalation channel, so applicants exhaust it without exploring DSO-based escalation paths
failure_patterns.consequence: Cases stall for months with only boilerplate responses while more effective escalation channels (DSO inquiry, congressional inquiry) go unused
warning_signs: Applicant has submitted two or more USCIS contact center inquiries with no substantive response
Applicant does not know that congressional inquiries are an option

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating OPT as equivalent to H-1B sponsorship when answering work authorization questions
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Candidates conflate all visa-related processes under the umbrella of 'sponsorship'; anxiety causes over-disclosure and inaccurate self-description
failure_patterns.consequence: Candidate incorrectly answers 'no' or 'sort of' to 'are you authorized to work?' on OPT, triggering unnecessary recruiter concern or disqualification
warning_signs: Saying 'I need sponsorship' when currently on valid OPT
Hesitating or hedging when asked about current work authorization

failure_patterns.pattern: Accepting immigration promises verbally without written documentation in the offer letter
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Employees feel it would be awkward or adversarial to demand written immigration commitments from a seemingly good-faith employer; social norms around new job offers discourage hard negotiations on terms perceived as secondary
failure_patterns.consequence: Employer rescinded sponsorship 18 months later with no legal recourse for the employee; employee left with 15 months of OPT runway and forced job search under time pressure
warning_signs: Sponsorship discussed only verbally during offer conversation, not included in offer letter
Employer says 'we always sponsor our OPT employees' without putting it in writing
Employee accepts offer without asking for written confirmation of immigration support

failure_patterns.pattern: Exceeding the 90-day aggregate unemployment limit on post-completion OPT without realizing the counter does not reset
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students intuitively treat each job gap as independent; the cumulative nature of the limit is counterintuitive and underemphasized by international offices
failure_patterns.consequence: OPT status becomes technically invalid; the student may be out of status without knowing it, risking future immigration consequences
warning_signs: Taking more than 60 days between jobs during OPT and not tracking the running total
Assuming the OPT EAD expiration date is the only compliance deadline

failure_patterns.pattern: Delegating CPT compliance tracking entirely to the DSO or university co-op office
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students reasonably assume the institution that approves CPT is responsible for flagging eligibility limits; the institutional incentive is to facilitate co-ops, not to protect individual OPT eligibility
failure_patterns.consequence: OPT eligibility lost permanently — in one reported case, by only 3 weeks of overage on a final-semester internship
warning_signs: DSO has never mentioned the 12-month CPT rule during any authorization
Student is completing a third or fourth CPT authorization without having personally summed their hours

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating 'USCIS hasn't explicitly banned Day 1 CPT' as a green light to use it
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students interpret the absence of an explicit prohibition as legal safety; the technical argument feels like a shield against future scrutiny
failure_patterns.consequence: H-1B RFEs years later demanding explanation of prior work authorization; in some cases denial; in worst cases retroactive unlawful presence and multi-year bars
warning_signs: Student is citing 'no explicit ban' as their primary justification
Student has not consulted an independent immigration attorney — only the school's DSO

failure_patterns.pattern: Registering only for the general H-1B cap when eligible for the master's cap
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Candidates with US master's degrees are unaware that the master's cap is a separate pool, or assume their employer's attorney handles it correctly without verifying
failure_patterns.consequence: Loss of a free additional lottery entry, reducing selection probability unnecessarily across all attempts
warning_signs: Candidate has never verified their master's cap eligibility with their attorney
Employer's attorney filed in prior years without confirming degree classification

failure_patterns.pattern: Attempting to structure a cap-exempt H-1B arrangement without an immigration attorney
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The strategy sounds conceptually simple (work partly for a university, file through them), leading people to underestimate the legal complexity of dual-employment petitions and USCIS compliance requirements
failure_patterns.consequence: Improperly structured arrangements can be flagged as fraudulent during audits, resulting in petition denial or more serious immigration consequences including potential bars on future filings
warning_signs: Person describes the plan as straightforward or asks if they can file themselves
No mention of involving an immigration attorney in the structure phase

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating the H-1B lottery as the only acceptable outcome and making no genuine backup plan
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Sunk cost and identity investment — years of study, sacrifice, and career-building in the US make alternatives feel like abandonment rather than options
failure_patterns.consequence: When lottery losses accumulate, the person has no emotionally acceptable alternative, amplifying desperation, degrading job negotiation posture, and increasing vulnerability to exploitative employers
warning_signs: Describes Canada or returning home only as 'last resort' or 'if I have no choice'
Has not actively researched backup options beyond knowing they exist
Frames H-1B selection as something they 'deserve' based on qualifications

failure_patterns.pattern: Defaulting to the US path without modeling GC backlog by country of birth
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The US is the default aspiration; salary is higher; the GC backlog is abstract and distant when you're a student — the lottery feels like a solvable near-term problem
failure_patterns.consequence: Indian/Chinese nationals spend 3-10 years on OPT/H-1B only to realize the GC wait is generational, then must pivot to Canada with fewer years of Canadian work experience and reduced CRS score potential
warning_signs: Student plans their career around 'eventually getting the GC' without checking current priority dates
No contingency plan discussed if H-1B lottery is lost 2+ consecutive times

failure_patterns.pattern: Employer submits I-983 with incorrect NAICS code and no clear STEM connection narrative, triggering an RFE
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: HR departments that have not processed STEM OPT before treat I-983 as a generic form rather than a substantive legal document requiring degree-to-role justification; the student defers to HR assuming they know what they are doing
failure_patterns.consequence: USCIS issues an RFE requiring supplemental evidence, adding weeks or months to processing and creating risk of work authorization lapse if the original OPT was close to expiry
warning_signs: HR says 'we've never done this before' or 'we'll figure it out'
I-983 submitted without DSO or attorney review
Job description in I-983 is copied from the offer letter rather than written to show STEM connection

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating the 90-day OPT unemployment limit as a per-episode cap rather than a cumulative total
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The word 'cap' implies a reset or per-incident limit; the cumulative nature is counterintuitive and not prominently communicated by universities
failure_patterns.consequence: Students exhaust their buffer earlier than expected, triggering an out-of-status situation without realizing it
warning_signs: Student has already had one employment gap of 45+ days
Student is between jobs and not actively tracking cumulative days
failure_patterns._source: zhihu_F_1留学生从OPT到H_1B_中间有哪些需要注意的关键节点.txt

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating the H-1B lottery as the only path and making no contingency plans during STEM OPT
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Optimism bias combined with the effort cost of researching alternatives; each lottery attempt feels like it might succeed, deferring backup planning indefinitely
failure_patterns.consequence: After three misses, the person is in a status crisis with no prepared alternatives, forcing rushed or suboptimal decisions (returning home, leaving the field, accepting worse roles)
warning_signs: Person is in second or third OPT year and has not researched O-1, NIW, or Canada PR
Person frames the question as 'what do I do after three misses' rather than 'what am I building in parallel'
failure_patterns._source: zhihu_H_1B三次抽签都没中的话_留在美国还有哪些合法途径.txt

failure_patterns.pattern: Deferring the parental reunion question entirely until after immigration status is secured
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Immigrants focus on their own status first (green card, PR) and treat parental reunion as a 'later problem'; by the time status is secured, the timeline constraints have already foreclosed good options
failure_patterns.consequence: Parents are already elderly when sponsorship becomes possible; the reunion window is either closed or severely compressed
warning_signs: Saying 'I'll figure out my parents' situation once I get my green card'
Not knowing whether your country of birth creates a 10+ year green card backlog
failure_patterns._source: zhihu_在美国还是加拿大工作_对国内父母将来的团聚有多大影响.txt

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating the H-1B lottery as a binary win/lose without modeling cumulative risk across all 3 STEM OPT attempts
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Each individual lottery feels like a coin-flip with decent odds (~35%); people mentally anchor on 'this draw' rather than the compound probability of losing all three
failure_patterns.consequence: ~27% of STEM OPT users exhaust all draws and are forced to leave the US mid-career, having not prepared a backup plan
warning_signs: Saying 'the odds are good enough' without calculating (0.65)^3
Not having a Canada or home-country contingency plan while on OPT
failure_patterns._source: zhihu_美国H_1B和加拿大PR_对于中国背景的留学生_哪条路更现实.txt

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating green card filing as an administrative formality rather than a multi-decade strategic commitment
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: H-1B approval feels like the hard part; green card feels like paperwork that follows naturally. The backlog reality is not visible until someone is already in it.
failure_patterns.consequence: Applicants discover 5-10 years in that their priority date has barely moved, by which point they are deeply embedded in US life (mortgages, children, aging parents) and have fewer exit options
warning_signs: Describing green card as 'just a process' or 'the next step after H-1B'
Not looking up current Visa Bulletin priority dates before accepting a job offer or planning a family timeline
failure_patterns._source: zhihu_美国绿卡排期对印度_中国大陆出生的申请人有多严重.txt

failure_patterns.pattern: Underweighting psychological cost of immigration uncertainty in career planning
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Salary and career opportunity are quantifiable; psychological stress is not, so it gets systematically discounted in rational-seeming analyses
failure_patterns.consequence: Years of high anxiety around H-1B renewal, lottery, job changes, and layoffs; decision paralysis during critical career moments
warning_signs: Framing the US vs. Canada decision purely in terms of salary and career opportunity
Dismissing 'stress' as a soft factor not worth quantifying
failure_patterns._source: canada_加拿大vs美国移民路线的真实对比_从两边都走过的角度.txt

failure_patterns.pattern: Waiting until after H1B lottery failure to start evaluating alternatives
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Optimism bias — each year feels like this might be the lucky year; planning for failure feels like giving up
failure_patterns.consequence: Alternative paths (L1 setup, EB1A preparation, Canada CRS optimization) require months to years of lead time; starting after the third or fourth lottery loss means years of additional delay
warning_signs: You are applying for H1B lottery without a documented backup plan
You have not checked your Canada CRS score or EB1A eligibility despite multiple lottery failures

failure_patterns.pattern: Refusing to seriously research or plan for alternative immigration paths until forced to by a failed lottery
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Equating Plan B research with admitting defeat — a cognitive reframe where considering alternatives feels like abandoning the primary goal
failure_patterns.consequence: When the H-1B fails, the person is in emotional crisis AND lacks any prepared alternative path, leading to rushed decisions under maximum stress
warning_signs: Saying 'I'll figure out Canada/going home if it comes to that'
Never having looked up PGWP eligibility or Express Entry CRS score despite multiple lottery failures
Experiencing the lottery result as binary (success = stay, failure = catastrophe) with no middle ground
failure_patterns._source: immigration_关于H_1B抽签后身份焦虑_说几句心里话.txt

failure_patterns.pattern: Assuming the 90-day unemployment limit applies per gap, not cumulatively
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The word 'limit' implies a single-instance rule; the cumulative nature is not intuitive and is not prominently communicated by most schools
failure_patterns.consequence: Students unknowingly exceed the total allowance by treating each gap as starting fresh; this was confirmed by a highly upvoted comment (134 likes) describing an H-1B RFE triggered by a 4-month gap
warning_signs: Saying 'I've only been unemployed for X days this time' without accounting for prior gaps
Not asking DSO for a running total of unemployment days
failure_patterns._source: us_visa_OPT期间换工作完整攻略_我踩过的所有坑.txt

failure_patterns.pattern: Confusing school address with personal mailing address when determining service center assignment
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Applicants assume their mailing address determines which service center handles their case, when it is actually the school's address
failure_patterns.consequence: Confusion about case status, incorrect center checked for processing time estimates, potential errors on forms
warning_signs: Applicant's school is in one state but personal mailing address is in another
Applicant checking processing times for the wrong service center

failure_patterns.pattern: Apologizing or hedging excessively when disclosing visa status
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Internalized stigma around being an international candidate; fear of rejection causes candidates to preemptively minimize themselves
failure_patterns.consequence: Candidate comes across as less confident and less desirable independent of the visa situation, per high-upvote community comment (534 upvotes)
warning_signs: Starting sponsorship disclosure with 'I'm sorry but...' or 'I know this might be an issue'
Volunteering that sponsorship 'might be a problem' before the recruiter has expressed any concern

failure_patterns.pattern: Assuming a company's reputation or size guarantees immigration follow-through
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students associate company prestige or scale with institutional reliability, assuming large or well-regarded companies won't change immigration policies mid-employment
failure_patterns.consequence: Sponsorship rescinded despite prior commitment; company policy changes are independent of company reputation
warning_signs: Reasoning like 'they're a big company, they sponsor everyone'
No independent verification of current sponsorship policy at time of offer

failure_patterns.pattern: Attempting to work or start a new job during the 60-day grace period after program end
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The grace period feels like a continuation of authorized stay, and students conflate the right to remain in the US with the right to work
failure_patterns.consequence: Unauthorized employment, which is a serious immigration violation with long-term bars to future status
warning_signs: Asking 'can I start at a new employer during my 60-day grace period?'
Framing the grace period as a job-search-and-start window rather than a transition-only window

failure_patterns.pattern: Accepting a STEM OPT employer without verifying E-Verify enrollment
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students focus on job quality, compensation, and sponsorship willingness — E-Verify enrollment is a bureaucratic checkbox that feels like a technicality rather than a hard legal barrier
failure_patterns.consequence: 24 months of authorized work time forfeited; student must find a new employer or exit the US
warning_signs: Employer is a small startup or non-US-facing company
Student has not explicitly asked HR about E-Verify enrollment before signing the offer

failure_patterns.pattern: Assuming risk is resolved once OPT or H-1B is granted
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Each immigration milestone feels like a clean slate; students assume USCIS has reviewed and accepted their full history upon approval
failure_patterns.consequence: RFEs or denials on subsequent petitions (e.g., H-1B extensions, green card) that reopen the Day 1 CPT question without warning
warning_signs: Student says 'I'm on OPT/H-1B now so I should be fine'
Student has not disclosed Day 1 CPT background to current employer's immigration attorney

failure_patterns.pattern: Using a general employment attorney instead of an immigration specialist for H-1B filings
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Employers use the same law firm for all employment matters; cost-cutting; assumption that any attorney can handle immigration paperwork
failure_patterns.consequence: Poorly documented specialty occupation claims, incorrect SOC codes, higher RFE rates, and preventable denials
warning_signs: Employer's law firm does not specialize in immigration
Attorney does not ask detailed questions about actual job duties when preparing the petition

failure_patterns.pattern: Assuming university affiliation automatically confers cap-exempt status on the employer
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The association between universities and cap-exempt status is widely known, but the legal test (primary purpose of the specific entity) is not; people anchor on the university connection rather than the statutory criteria
failure_patterns.consequence: Pursuing a cap-exempt strategy through an ineligible entity (e.g., a for-profit spinout or a primarily clinical hospital) wastes time and money and may leave the person without a valid status path
warning_signs: Person says 'my employer is affiliated with [University]' without specifying the entity type
No inquiry into whether the entity's primary purpose is research

failure_patterns.pattern: Underestimating the chronic stress accumulation of multi-year visa uncertainty
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Year-1 optimism bias — first-time lottery entrants assume selection is likely or that they will cope fine with a loss; the emotional cost of sustained uncertainty is not visible until experienced
failure_patterns.consequence: By year 2–3 without a support structure (therapy, community, genuine backup plan), multiple commenters describe depression and fundamental questioning of whether the US path is worth continuing
warning_signs: Plans to 'cross that bridge when I come to it' on backup options
Has not discussed immigration stress with a mental health professional or peer community
Measures wellbeing primarily through visa status milestones

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating H-1B lottery as a near-certain outcome given enough attempts
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Availability heuristic: students see peers who won and assume persistence leads to success; individual lottery odds feel more favorable than they are
failure_patterns.consequence: Exhausts STEM OPT years waiting for H-1B that never comes; forced departure from US with no status and no PR in any country
warning_signs: Person frames lottery losses as 'bad luck' rather than recalibrating strategy
No alternative pathway being actively pursued after first or second loss

failure_patterns.pattern: Employee stops working when EAD card does not arrive before OPT expiry, not knowing timely-filed protection applies
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Neither the employee nor the employer knows that a receipt notice obtained before expiry allows continued employment — employer panics about I-9 compliance and instructs employee to stop
failure_patterns.consequence: Unnecessary employment gap, lost income, potential damage to work record — all avoidable with knowledge of the timely-filed rule
warning_signs: Employer HR asks for the new EAD card itself rather than accepting the receipt notice
Employee has not proactively informed HR of timely-filed protections before expiry date approaches

failure_patterns.pattern: Submitting a copy-paste generic I-983 Training Plan
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students perceive the I-983 as a bureaucratic checkbox rather than a substantive document; templates are widely shared in communities
failure_patterns.consequence: USCIS issues a NOID, requiring significant time and legal resources to respond, and potentially denying the STEM OPT extension
warning_signs: Student downloaded an I-983 template from a forum or peer
Training plan descriptions are vague or not tied to specific job duties
failure_patterns._source: zhihu_F_1留学生从OPT到H_1B_中间有哪些需要注意的关键节点.txt

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating US green card holder parent sponsorship as equivalent to citizen sponsorship
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: People conflate the two categories; both can sponsor parents, so they assume the process is similar
failure_patterns.consequence: Green card holders face a numerically-limited preference category with 5–10+ year backlogs vs. the immediate relative (no cap) category available to citizens — a critical difference
warning_signs: Statements like 'once I get my green card I can bring my parents'
Not knowing the difference between IR-5 and F4 preference categories
failure_patterns._source: zhihu_在美国还是加拿大工作_对国内父母将来的团聚有多大影响.txt

failure_patterns.pattern: India-born applicants entering EB-2 queue without modeling realistic wait time
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Priority date information exists publicly but requires active lookup and extrapolation; most people rely on word-of-mouth from slightly earlier cohorts whose wait times were shorter
failure_patterns.consequence: Committing to a US career path built around green card arrival that is realistically 40-50 years away, leading to either career paralysis or late-stage pivot to Canada with accumulated opportunity cost
warning_signs: Asking 'how long does EB-2 take' without specifying country of birth
Assuming EB-2 wait time is similar to what colleagues from other countries experienced
failure_patterns._source: zhihu_美国绿卡排期对印度_中国大陆出生的申请人有多严重.txt

failure_patterns.pattern: H-1B lottery failure leading to rushed or suboptimal decisions
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Two or more consecutive lottery failures create panic; workers feel they must take any available option rather than plan strategically
failure_patterns.consequence: Accepting bad job offers, leaving the US prematurely, or pivoting to Canada without proper preparation
warning_signs: Entering third H-1B lottery without a backup plan
No Canada work authorization or job pipeline developed during STEM OPT period
failure_patterns._source: canada_加拿大vs美国移民路线的真实对比_从两边都走过的角度.txt

failure_patterns.pattern: Delaying SEVIS update after starting a new job during OPT
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students focus on onboarding at the new job and deprioritize administrative tasks; the 10-day window is shorter than most bureaucratic timelines feel
failure_patterns.consequence: SEVIS record remains inaccurate, creating a compliance gap that can affect H-1B petitions or future visa applications
warning_signs: Starting a new job without immediately contacting DSO
Assuming the new employer handles the SEVIS update
failure_patterns._source: us_visa_OPT期间换工作完整攻略_我踩过的所有坑.txt

failure_patterns.pattern: Not raising H-1B dependency until after receiving an offer
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Candidates fear early rejection and delay the conversation as long as possible, confusing 'later is safer' with 'later is better'
failure_patterns.consequence: Offer may be rescinded, or candidate must negotiate under pressure; some companies feel misled, creating reputational risk
warning_signs: Planning to 'deal with it when I get the offer'
Not having asked about sponsorship policy before final round

failure_patterns.pattern: Assuming cap-gap coverage without checking EAD expiration timing
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The cap-gap rule is widely discussed but the precondition (petition must be filed before OPT expires) is frequently omitted in community explanations, creating false confidence
failure_patterns.consequence: Student falls out of status between OPT expiration and H-1B start date, potentially triggering unlawful presence
warning_signs: Student knows they applied for H-1B but has not checked whether their EAD will still be valid on April 1
Student describes cap-gap as 'automatic' without referencing petition timing

failure_patterns.pattern: Not asking HR about cap-exempt bridge options until after lottery loss
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Employees assume only direct H-1B sponsorship is available; HR departments rarely proactively disclose internal transfer or affiliated entity options
failure_patterns.consequence: Missing the window to arrange a cap-exempt bridge, forcing departure or career disruption
warning_signs: Employer has never mentioned cap-exempt options
Employee has never asked HR whether the company has nonprofit or university-affiliated entities

failure_patterns.pattern: Assuming H-1B sponsorship is implicitly agreed to without an explicit early conversation
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students conflate an employer being 'visa-friendly' with a firm commitment to sponsor; the topic feels awkward to raise early
failure_patterns.consequence: By the time the student raises H-1B in year two or three, internal processes, budget freezes, or policy changes prevent the employer from committing in time for the registration window
warning_signs: Student has never explicitly discussed H-1B with HR or manager
Student is in final year of STEM OPT without a confirmed sponsor
failure_patterns._source: zhihu_F_1留学生从OPT到H_1B_中间有哪些需要注意的关键节点.txt

failure_patterns.pattern: Not evaluating EB-1A until too late in career to accumulate qualifying evidence
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: EB-1A is perceived as 'for Nobel Prize winners' — the community widely underestimates who qualifies; early-career researchers don't build toward it because they don't know it's achievable
failure_patterns.consequence: Missed window to accumulate publications, citations, media coverage, and peer review credits that would have made EB-1A viable — instead stuck in EB-2 backlog
warning_signs: Dismissing EB-1A as 'not for me' without consulting an immigration attorney
No awareness that EB-1A requires no employer sponsorship or PERM
failure_patterns._source: zhihu_美国绿卡排期对印度_中国大陆出生的申请人有多严重.txt

failure_patterns.pattern: Starting STEM OPT work before I-983 and E-Verify are complete
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: New employers often do not know the pre-start requirement; students assume documentation can be completed during onboarding like I-9 paperwork
failure_patterns.consequence: Work performed before documentation is in place may be considered unauthorized employment under STEM OPT rules
warning_signs: Employer says 'we'll sort the paperwork out once you start'
I-983 not signed before the first day of work
failure_patterns._source: us_visa_OPT期间换工作完整攻略_我踩过的所有坑.txt

diagnostic_questions.question: Has this F-1 student ever been authorized for full-time CPT, and if so, for how many cumulative months?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student has already forfeited OPT eligibility or is approaching the 12-month threshold that would eliminate it.
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Calculate total cumulative full-time CPT months. If at or near 12, advise the student to switch to part-time CPT or discontinue CPT entirely to protect OPT.
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Confirm the student's CPT history is clean and OPT is fully available. Proceed with standard OPT planning.

diagnostic_questions.question: Did you obtain written DSO authorization in SEVIS before dropping below full-time enrollment — or did you drop first and contact the DSO after?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student is currently in status or already out of status — this single sequencing question determines the entire remediation path
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Which of the 4 RCL exceptions was cited, and is the supporting documentation (e.g., medical records) on file with the DSO?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: How many days ago did the drop occur? You may be in a reinstatement scenario — calculate time elapsed against the 5-month window immediately

diagnostic_questions.question: How many cumulative months of full-time CPT have you used across all semesters?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student has already permanently lost OPT eligibility or is approaching the threshold that would eliminate it
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If 12 or more months: OPT is permanently gone — what alternative status arrangements are in place for post-graduation work authorization?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If under 12 months: how many more months of full-time CPT are planned, and will the total remain under 12?

diagnostic_questions.question: What is the exact date your academic requirements were fulfilled — not your graduation ceremony date?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student understands when their 60-day grace period actually started and whether they may already be accruing unlawful presence
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Have you confirmed this date with your registrar and DSO, and have you already filed or initiated your OPT application?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Contact your registrar immediately to get the official program completion date before making any other immigration decision

diagnostic_questions.question: Have you moved to a new address at any point during your program without notifying your DSO within 15 days (F-1) or 10 days (J-1)?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether a SEVIS address reporting violation exists that could surface as an RFE or denial during OPT, H-1B, or green card processing
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: When exactly did you move, and when did you notify your DSO? Calculate the gap and assess whether it exceeds the reporting window; if so, consult a DSO or attorney immediately
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Verify by cross-referencing DSO records with actual move dates — students often underestimate or misremember the reporting timeline

diagnostic_questions.question: Can you name your exact current visa status expiration date and the deadline for filing your next status transition?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student is actively managing their immigration timeline or operating reactively
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: What is your Plan B if your primary transition pathway fails — lottery loss, employer withdrawal, or denial?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: This is the most urgent priority before any other career or immigration planning; find out immediately and set calendar reminders 6 and 12 months in advance

diagnostic_questions.question: What is your country of birth?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Determines whether the EB-2 pathway is viable within a career-relevant timeframe or is effectively unusable under current per-country quota rules
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If India or China: ask 'Have you checked the current Visa Bulletin Final Action Date for EB-2 for your country, and are you prepared for a potentially multi-decade wait?'
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If other country: EB-2 is likely current or near-current — focus shifts to PERM vs. NIW track selection and employer sponsorship feasibility

diagnostic_questions.question: Does your offered salary meet DOL OEWS Level III or Level IV thresholds for your occupation and location?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether you will receive 3–4 lottery entries (strong odds) or only 1–2 (weak odds) under the wage-based selection system
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Confirm employer will register at the highest eligible OEWS level, not just the job title's assumed level
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Evaluate whether salary negotiation is possible, or whether a cap-exempt employer is a better path

diagnostic_questions.question: Is your exact degree program listed on the current DHS STEM Designated Degree Program List?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student has 12 months or 36 months of OPT eligibility, which directly determines how many H-1B lottery attempts they have.
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Has your employer confirmed they are E-Verify enrolled, which is required for STEM OPT extension?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: What is your contingency plan given that you have only one H-1B lottery attempt before status expires?

diagnostic_questions.question: Has the student's DSO already recommended OPT in SEVIS, and if so, when?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the 30-day I-765 filing clock is already running and how much time remains before automatic denial
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Calculate exact days remaining in the 30-day window and prioritize immediate I-765 submission over all other application steps
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Determine how far from graduation the student is and whether the 90-day early filing window is open yet

diagnostic_questions.question: Is the reason this student wants to drop below full-time enrollment one of the four permitted RCL exceptions?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether a reduced course load can be legally authorized or whether the student must maintain full enrollment regardless of personal circumstances.
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Document the exception, obtain required supporting materials (medical documentation, academic advisor letter, etc.), and authorize RCL in SEVIS before the drop occurs.
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Advise the student they cannot drop below full-time. Explore alternatives: adding a course at another CUNY school, concurrent enrollment, or transferring temporarily to a school where full-time enrollment is feasible.

diagnostic_questions.question: How long ago did the status violation begin — and has it been more than 5 months?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student qualifies for standard USCIS reinstatement or must meet the much harder 'exceptional circumstances' standard
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Document any exceptional circumstances carefully; consult an immigration attorney immediately — standard reinstatement is likely unavailable
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: File reinstatement application as soon as possible; every additional day of delay reduces the window further

diagnostic_questions.question: Is your employer currently enrolled in E-Verify — not just willing to enroll, but actively enrolled right now?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the STEM OPT extension is actually available with this employer at time of filing, or whether the student will be limited to 12-month standard OPT
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If yes: confirm enrollment with HR in writing and note the employer's E-Verify company ID for the STEM extension application
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If no or uncertain: assess whether employer will enroll before STEM OPT filing deadline, and evaluate alternative employers who are enrolled

diagnostic_questions.question: How many cumulative unemployment days have you used since your OPT start date?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student is at risk of automatic OPT termination and whether they have enough buffer to conduct a job search or change employers
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If under 60 days used, proceed with job search but set a hard deadline to secure employment before day 80; if over 75 days, escalate to DSO immediately
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If student cannot answer, they are not tracking — treat as high risk and reconstruct the count from employment records immediately

diagnostic_questions.question: Did you perform any compensated work — formal or informal — before your EAD card was physically in your possession?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether an unauthorized employment violation exists in the student's SEVIS record, which is one of the most severe and least recoverable F-1 violations
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Consult an immigration attorney immediately; determine whether reinstatement is still possible and assess the impact on pending or future immigration filings
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Confirm the student understands that approval notice ≠ work authorization; the physical EAD card must be received before any work begins

diagnostic_questions.question: When you say 'my employer will sponsor me,' what specifically have they told you about their immigration attorney, filing timeline, and internal process?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the sponsorship commitment is operationally real or a well-intentioned but unexecuted assurance
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Have they successfully sponsored someone at your visa category before — and can you speak to that person?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Treat the sponsorship as unconfirmed; ask the specific operational questions before relying on this employer as your primary immigration pathway

diagnostic_questions.question: Do you have a PhD or equivalent research profile with publications, citations, or patents?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Determines NIW eligibility — if yes, self-petition may be viable, eliminating employer dependency and PERM timeline entirely
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Assess evidence strength against all 6 NIW evidence types — if 3+ are strongly documentable, NIW is worth pursuing with attorney review
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Pivot to employer-sponsored PERM track — assess employer willingness and whether Exceptional Ability category (3 of 6 evidence types) is achievable

diagnostic_questions.question: Is your employer a cap-exempt organization (university, nonprofit research org, government research org)?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the H-1B lottery applies to you at all — cap-exempt employers can hire you year-round without lottery participation
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Confirm the specific exemption category and ensure the petition cites it correctly; no lottery preparation needed
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Proceed with lottery strategy; assess salary level, registration timing, and alternative pathways

diagnostic_questions.question: Does your employer have cap-exempt status — specifically, are they a university, university-affiliated nonprofit, or government research organization?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the lottery is even relevant — cap-exempt employers can file H-1B year-round, eliminating the primary bottleneck in the US immigration funnel.
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Have they successfully filed cap-exempt H-1B petitions before, and are you aware of the constraint of remaining with that employer?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: What wage level will your position be classified at, and how does that affect your lottery selection odds under the new system?

diagnostic_questions.question: Does the student's degree CIP code appear on the DHS STEM Designated Degree Program list?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student is eligible for the 24-month STEM OPT extension, which changes the entire timeline and strategy for transitioning to H-1B
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Confirm E-Verify enrollment of any potential employers and begin STEM OPT extension application during the last 90 days of regular OPT
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Student has only 12 months of OPT; H-1B strategy must account for a much tighter window and higher urgency to secure sponsoring employer

diagnostic_questions.question: Is this J-1 student's primary source of funding from a government, university, or international organization?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student meets J-1 visa program funding requirements, and whether they may be subject to the 212(e) two-year home residency requirement.
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Confirm the funding source is listed as primary on the DS-2019. Check whether the program or funding source triggers 212(e). If so, advise the student early about post-program pathway limitations.
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Determine if the student qualifies for J-1 at all, since individual/personal funds can only be secondary. If not eligible for J-1, explore F-1 as an alternative status.

diagnostic_questions.question: Does your situation fit one of the 4 federally defined RCL exceptions: academic difficulty, medical condition, final semester, or improper course level placement?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether DSO authorization for reduced course load is even legally available — if the answer is no, the student cannot legally drop below full-time regardless of the hardship
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Do you have the required documentation ready (e.g., licensed physician letter for medical RCL) to present to the DSO before submitting the course drop?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: The student must maintain full-time enrollment or explore other options (medical leave of absence, program withdrawal, status change) — RCL is not available

diagnostic_questions.question: What is the start date printed on your EAD card, and have you begun working yet?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether unemployment days are accumulating and how much of the 90-day buffer remains before a status violation occurs
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If working: confirm the employer's position qualifies as related to the field of study for OPT compliance
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If not working: calculate days elapsed since EAD start date and determine urgency of securing employment

diagnostic_questions.question: Is your current or prospective employer enrolled in E-Verify?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student is eligible for STEM OPT extension, which determines if they have 12 or 36 total months of OPT work authorization
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Confirm STEM degree CIP code eligibility and begin STEM extension application before current OPT expires
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Assess whether employer can enroll in E-Verify; if not, evaluate whether a different employer or freelance arrangement is possible before OPT expires

diagnostic_questions.question: Are you on J-1 status, and is your health insurance currently active and continuously maintained?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student is at risk of the single most unrecoverable J-1 violation; health insurance is federally mandated for J-1, and willful lapses permanently terminate status
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Confirm the coverage meets J-1 federal requirements (minimum coverage limits, repatriation, medical evacuation); not all health insurance qualifies
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Assess whether the lapse is willful; if so, the student must consult an immigration attorney immediately as reinstatement is not available for willful J-1 health insurance failures

diagnostic_questions.question: Are you staying in this country because you genuinely believe it compounds better for you over 10 years, or because returning home would feel like failure?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the stay-vs-return decision is analytically grounded or emotionally driven by social framing of return as defeat
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Can you articulate specifically which career, financial, or personal factors compound better here — and have you run the purchasing power comparison?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: The decision warrants a structured reanalysis using a 10-year compounding framework before committing to another multi-year immigration cycle

diagnostic_questions.question: Is your employer willing to commit in writing to sponsoring your PERM labor certification?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Determines whether the employer-sponsored EB-2 track is a real option or theoretical — many employers verbally indicate willingness but refuse when the process begins
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Proceed to assess PERM timeline, I-140 processing strategy, and priority date implications for your country
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: NIW self-petition becomes the primary pathway — assess eligibility rigorously; also evaluate Canada as an alternative if NIW is not viable
